title: Visit Japan Web - Pre-Flight Checklist
text: Visit Japan Web is an official digital platform provided by the Japanese government that lets tourists pre-register arrival procedures—including immigration, customs, and tax-free declarations—online before arriving in Japan.

title: Visit Japan Web - Pre-Flight Checklist
text: Visit Japan Web is an official digital platform provided by the Japanese government that lets tourists pre-register arrival procedures—including immigration, customs, and tax-free declarations—online before arriving in Japan.

title: Visit Japan Web - Pre-Flight Checklist
text:
Is it Required?
Visit Japan Web has never been “required” for immigration and customs, though it has always been strongly recommended. However, many airlines have stopped handing out arrival cards and customs declarations before you land, making online registration the most convenient option.
Bottom line: Not mandatory, but highly recommended to speed up your arrival process significantly.

title: Visit Japan Web - Pre-Flight Checklist
text:
What You’ll Need
Before starting, gather these documents:

Valid passport
Flight details (airline, flight number, departure city)
Accommodation information in Japan (hotel name, address, phone number)
Email address (for account creation)

Note: As of April 29, 2023, Japan has lifted all COVID-19 border measures. No vaccination certificate, negative test result, or quarantine is required.

title: Visit Japan Web - Pre-Flight Checklist
text:
Registration Timeline
Recommended: Complete your registration at home, about two weeks before your departure (around October 17, 2025).
Time required: About 15 minutes

title: Visit Japan Web - Pre-Flight Checklist
text:
Step-by-Step Process

Step 1: Create Account

Go to https://services.digital.go.jp/en/visit-japan-web/
Register with email address and password
Verify your email



Step 2: Enter Your Details

Click “Your Details” on home screen
Enter passport information (name, number, nationality, date of birth, gender)
Add family members if traveling together (up to 10 people on same itinerary)



Step 3: Register Your Trip

Click “Register new planned entry/return”
Enter trip details:

Trip name: “October 31 Tokyo”
Arrival date: October 31, 2025
Airline: Your airline name/code
Flight number: Enter numbers only (if flight is NH0123, enter “0123”)
Departure city: Incheon
Accommodation details in Tokyo




Step 4: Complete Immigration & Customs Forms
Visit Japan Web consists of three parts: immigration information (disembarkation card), customs information (customs declaration), and tax-exemption section.
Fill out: - Immigration/disembarkation card - Customs declaration


Step 5: Generate QR Codes

Review all information carefully
Click “Display QR Code”
Click “Agree and display the QR Code”
Important: A screenshot or printed version of the QR code is not accepted. You’ll need a phone with internet access (via airport Wi-Fi) or have the offline Visit Japan Web app installed.

title: Visit Japan Web - Pre-Flight Checklist
text:
At the Airport (October 31)
What to bring: - Your phone with Visit Japan Web QR codes displayed - Ensure you have internet access (airport WiFi) or download the offline app
At Tokyo Airport: 1. Show QR code at immigration desk 2. Provide fingerprints and facial photo (standard for all visitors) 3. Show QR code at customs 4. Using Visit Japan Web affords you access to a dedicated customs queue, which you can often clear in just seconds

title: Visit Japan Web - Pre-Flight Checklist
text:
Important Reminders

Complete registration before October 31
Ensure your phone is charged
Have airport WiFi access or offline app ready
Once you click to display the QR code, you can no longer modify trip information
Double-check all information before generating QR codes

title: Visit Japan Web - Pre-Flight Checklist
section: Benefits of Using Visit Japan Web
text:

Skip filling out paper forms
Faster immigration and customs processing
Dedicated express lanes at the airport
All information submitted in advance

title: Tailscale
text: Great question! Since your Fedora Linux machine is already on Tailscale, routing through your exit node is straightforward.

title: Tailscale
section: Enable Exit Node on Fedora Linux
text:
Enable Exit Node on Fedora Linux

Quick Command
sudo tailscale up --exit-node=<exit-node-name>
Replace <exit-node-name> with your home machine’s Tailscale name.

title: Tailscale
text:
Step-by-Step

1. Find Your Exit Node Name
tailscale status
Look for your home machine in the list - it should show something like exit-node or your machine name.
Or list available exit nodes:
tailscale exit-node list


2. Connect to Exit Node
sudo tailscale up --exit-node=your-home-machine-name
Example:
sudo tailscale up --exit-node=desktop-home
Or use the Tailscale IP:
sudo tailscale up --exit-node=100.x.x.x


3. Verify It’s Working
curl ifconfig.me
This should show your home IP address, not your current location’s IP.
Or check Tailscale status:
tailscale status
Should show you’re using the exit node.

title: Tailscale
text:
Disable Exit Node
When you want to stop routing through exit node:
sudo tailscale up --exit-node=
(Empty value disables it)

title: Tailscale
text:
Make It Persistent
If you want it to always use the exit node on boot:
sudo tailscale up --exit-node=your-home-machine --exit-node-allow-lan-access
The --exit-node-allow-lan-access flag lets you still access local network devices while using exit node.

title: Tailscale
text:
GUI Option (If Available)
Some Linux distributions have Tailscale GUI. If you have it: 1. Click Tailscale icon in system tray 2. Look for “Use exit node” option 3. Select your home machine
But command line is usually fastest on Linux.

title: Tailscale
text:
Troubleshooting
“exit node is not advertising exit node capability” - Make sure your home machine is actually advertising as exit node - On home machine: sudo tailscale up --advertise-exit-node
Permission denied - Need sudo for tailscale commands
Can’t connect - Check: tailscale status - is everything connected? - Verify home machine is online and advertising as exit node
DNS issues - Try: sudo tailscale up --exit-node=your-node --accept-dns

title: Tailscale
text:
Quick Reference
# Connect to exit node
sudo tailscale up --exit-node=home-machine

# Disconnect from exit node  
sudo tailscale up --exit-node=

# Check status
tailscale status

# List available exit nodes
tailscale exit-node list

# Check your public IP
curl ifconfig.me

That’s it! On Linux it’s actually simpler than Windows - just one command. Let me know if you run into any issues!
Ready to jump back into Korea/Japan planning when you are.

title: The Korean War & The Incheon Landing
text:
The Korean War was a devastating conflict that shaped modern Korea and remains technically unresolved today—the two Koreas are still separated by an armistice, not a peace treaty.


Division of Korea (1945) - At the end of World War II, Korea was liberated from Japanese colonial rule - The peninsula was divided at the 38th parallel: Soviet forces occupied the north, American forces the south - This temporary division became permanent as Cold War tensions escalated - Two separate governments formed: communist North Korea (DPRK) under Kim Il-sung, and South Korea (ROK) under Syngman Rhee.
The Invasion (June 25, 1950) - North Korean forces crossed the 38th parallel and invaded South Korea - The attack was sudden and overwhelming, with Soviet-supplied tanks and weapons - South Korean and initial US forces were pushed back rapidly - Within weeks, UN/South Korean forces were trapped in a small perimeter around the port city of Pusan (now Busan) in the southeast corner.



By August 1950, the situation was desperate: - North Korean forces controlled 90% of the Korean peninsula - UN forces (primarily American and South Korean) held only a small defensive perimeter around Pusan - If Pusan fell, UN forces would be pushed into the sea - The war appeared nearly lost

title: The Korean War & The Incheon Landing
text:
The Korean War was a devastating conflict that shaped modern Korea and remains technically unresolved today—the two Koreas are still separated by an armistice, not a peace treaty.


Division of Korea (1945) - At the end of World War II, Korea was liberated from Japanese colonial rule - The peninsula was divided at the 38th parallel: Soviet forces occupied the north, American forces the south - This temporary division became permanent as Cold War tensions escalated - Two separate governments formed: communist North Korea (DPRK) under Kim Il-sung, and South Korea (ROK) under Syngman Rhee.
The Invasion (June 25, 1950) - North Korean forces crossed the 38th parallel and invaded South Korea - The attack was sudden and overwhelming, with Soviet-supplied tanks and weapons - South Korean and initial US forces were pushed back rapidly - Within weeks, UN/South Korean forces were trapped in a small perimeter around the port city of Pusan (now Busan) in the southeast corner.



By August 1950, the situation was desperate: - North Korean forces controlled 90% of the Korean peninsula - UN forces (primarily American and South Korean) held only a small defensive perimeter around Pusan - If Pusan fell, UN forces would be pushed into the sea - The war appeared nearly lost

title: The Korean War & The Incheon Landing
text:
The Incheon Landing: MacArthur’s Masterstroke

The Audacious Plan
General Douglas MacArthur, commander of UN forces, proposed a daring amphibious assault at Incheon, a port city on Korea’s western coast near Seoul.
Why Incheon was considered impossible: - Extreme tidal variations (30+ feet) - among the highest in the world - Narrow, winding approach channels that could trap ships - Only a few days per month when tides allowed landing - Heavily fortified and far behind enemy lines - Most military planners called it a suicide mission
Why MacArthur chose it anyway: - It was precisely because it seemed impossible that North Korean forces were unprepared - Success would cut North Korean supply lines in half - Seoul (the capital) was only 20 miles away - It would trap North Korean forces between two fronts.


Operation Chromite (September 15, 1950)
The Landing - 75,000 troops and 261 naval vessels assembled in secret - The landing began at dawn on September 15, 1950 - Marines had to scale seawalls using ladders because of extreme tides - Initial resistance was lighter than expected—the surprise was complete - Within hours, Incheon was secured
Immediate Impact - North Korean forces were caught completely off guard - Their supply lines from the north to the Pusan front were severed - UN forces broke out of the Pusan perimeter simultaneously - North Korean army found itself surrounded and began collapsing.
Liberation of Seoul - By September 26, Seoul was recaptured after intense street fighting - North Korean forces fled northward in disarray - The war’s momentum completely reversed in just 11 days


The Turning Point
The Incheon landing is considered one of the most brilliant military operations in modern history: - Transformed a near-defeat into sudden victory - Saved South Korea from complete conquest - Demonstrated MacArthur’s strategic genius (and considerable ego) - Changed the entire course of the war.

title: The Korean War & The Incheon Landing
text:
What Happened Next
UN Advance North (Fall 1950) - Emboldened by success, UN forces pushed north past the 38th parallel - By October, they were approaching the Chinese border - MacArthur believed the war was nearly won.
Chinese Intervention (November 1950) - China warned against approaching its border but was ignored - Hundreds of thousands of Chinese “volunteers” entered the war - UN forces were driven back south in bitter winter fighting - Seoul changed hands again.
Stalemate (1951-1953) - The front lines stabilized roughly along the 38th parallel - Trench warfare and negotiations dragged on for two more years - MacArthur was fired by President Truman in April 1951 for insubordination (he wanted to expand the war into China) - Armistice signed July 27, 1953—but no peace treaty.

title: The Korean War & The Incheon Landing
text:
Legacy and Impact
Human Cost - Approximately 3 million Korean civilians killed - 1+ million combined military casualties - Entire cities destroyed, families separated - Korea remains divided today along the DMZ (Demilitarized Zone).
Modern Significance - The war created the world’s most heavily fortified border - North and South Korea developed into radically different societies - Millions of Korean families remain separated - The “Forgotten War” in America, but defining trauma in Korea - Technically still at war—only an armistice, not peace.
Visiting Historical Sites Today - Incheon Landing Memorial Hall - Museum and monuments at the landing site - Freedom Bridge - Symbol of the divided peninsula - War Memorial of Korea (Seoul) - Comprehensive museum - DMZ Tours - Visit the demilitarized zone separating North and South.

title: The Korean War & The Incheon Landing
section: Why This Matters for Your Visit
text:
Why This Matters for Your Visit
Understanding the Korean War helps contextualize modern Korea: - Why Korea is divided - The generation that rebuilt South Korea from ashes - Korea’s relationship with the United States - The ongoing tensions with North Korea - The resilience and determination of Korean people - Why military service is mandatory for Korean men.
The Incheon landing, in particular, represents a moment when history pivoted—when catastrophic defeat transformed into unexpected victory, shaping the Korean peninsula for decades to come.

The Korean War is sometimes called “The Forgotten War” in the West, but for Koreans, it remains the defining event of modern history. Every family has a story from the war.

title: Korea & Japan 2025
text:
We’re embarking on an incredible journey through two of East Asia’s most fascinating countries—South Korea and Japan. This site documents our planning, shares our itinerary, and will serve as a travel journal as we explore ancient temples, modern cities, incredible food, and rich cultural traditions.
The journey is made even more special by meaningful connections: in Korea, we’ll be reuniting with good friends who call Seoul home, getting an insider’s perspective on Korean life and culture. Then we’ll join the Wisconsin Quilt Museum’s tour group for an art-focused exploration of Japan, diving deep into textile traditions and contemporary creativity.

Then we’ll join the Wisconsin Quilt Museum’s tour group for an art-focused exploration of Japan, diving deep into textile traditions and contemporary creativity.

title: Korea & Japan 2025
text:
Why Korea & Japan?
These two neighboring countries offer a perfect blend of ancient history and cutting-edge modernity. From Seoul’s vibrant neighborhoods and palaces to Kyoto’s serene temples, from Korean BBQ to fresh sushi, from traditional tea ceremonies to neon-lit streets—we’re ready to experience it all.
The trip combines two distinct experiences: we’ll start in Korea, where we have dear friends who will show us their country through local eyes, followed by a guided cultural and historical tour. Then we’ll continue to Japan as part of the Wisconsin Quilt Museum’s art and culture tour led by Heidi Parkes, exploring Japanese textile traditions, contemporary art, and cultural sites.

title: Korea & Japan 2025
text:
What You’ll Find Here
Things to See & Do - A curated list of attractions, restaurants, experiences, and hidden gems we’re considering for each destination. This is our master planning list as we finalize our itinerary.
Blog - Real-time updates, photos, and stories as we travel. We’ll share what we’re experiencing, what worked (and what didn’t), and the unexpected discoveries along the way.
Korea Trip Overview and Japan Schedule - High-level summaries of each portion of the trip.
Day-by-Day Schedules - Use the left sidebar navigation to dive into detailed daily itineraries, including cities, accommodations, planned activities, and our notes as we travel. This is where you’ll find the nitty-gritty of what we’re doing each day.

title: Korea & Japan 2025
text:
Our Approach
We’re fortunate to experience these countries through multiple lenses: the local knowledge of friends in Korea, structured cultural tours, and the Wisconsin Quilt Museum’s carefully curated art itinerary in Japan. We’re blending guided experiences with free time to wander, mixing famous landmarks with local hangouts, and leaving room for spontaneity. The goal is to immerse ourselves in the culture, try as much local food as possible, and return with stories worth telling.
This site is equal parts planning tool and travel journal—a living document that will grow and evolve as we prepare for and embark on this adventure.

Follow along as we explore Korea and Japan. We’ll see you on the other side of the Pacific!

title: A Guide to Japanese Food Categories
text: Japanese cuisine, or washoku, is recognized by UNESCO as an Intangible Cultural Heritage. It emphasizes seasonality, quality of ingredients, presentation, and balance. The philosophy centers on bringing out the natural flavors of ingredients with minimal processing. This guide covers the major categories of Japanese food you’ll encounter during your November trip.

title: A Guide to Japanese Food Categories
text:
Sushi & Sashimi

Sushi (寿司)
Vinegared rice combined with various ingredients, most commonly raw fish. The art of sushi requires years of training.
Major styles: - Nigiri - hand-pressed rice topped with fish or seafood - Maki - rolled sushi wrapped in nori (seaweed) - Hosomaki - thin rolls with one ingredient - Futomaki - thick rolls with multiple ingredients - Uramaki - inside-out rolls (rice on outside) - Temaki - hand-rolled cone shape - Chirashi - scattered sushi (sashimi over rice in a bowl) - Oshi-zushi - pressed sushi (Osaka specialty) - Inari-zushi - sushi rice in sweet fried tofu pouches
Common toppings (neta): - Maguro - tuna (chu-toro = medium fatty, o-toro = very fatty) - Sake - salmon - Ebi - shrimp - Tako - octopus - Ika - squid - Uni - sea urchin - Ikura - salmon roe - Tamago - sweet egg omelet


Sashimi (刺身)
Fresh raw fish or seafood sliced and served without rice. Typically served with wasabi, soy sauce, and garnishes like daikon radish and shiso leaves.
Quality indicators: - Freshness is paramount - Fish should have a clean ocean smell, never “fishy” - Texture should be firm but melt-in-your-mouth - Served at optimal temperature for each fish


Sushi Etiquette

Use your hands or chopsticks (both acceptable)
Dip fish side into soy sauce, not the rice
Eat nigiri in one bite if possible
Ginger (gari) is a palate cleanser between pieces
Don’t mix wasabi into soy sauce at high-end restaurants
crumbs: A Guide to Japanese Food Categories

title: A Guide to Japanese Food Categories
text:
Noodles (Men)

Ramen (ラーメン)
Chinese-style wheat noodles in meat or fish broth, topped with various ingredients. One of Japan’s most popular dishes.
Major broth styles: - Shoyu - soy sauce base (Tokyo style) - Miso - fermented soybean paste base (Hokkaido specialty) - Shio - salt base (light and clear) - Tonkotsu - pork bone base (Kyushu specialty, milky white) - Tori paitan - chicken base (creamy)
Common toppings: - Chashu - braised pork slices - Menma - fermented bamboo shoots - Negi - green onions - Ajitsuke tamago - marinated soft-boiled egg - Nori - dried seaweed - Moyashi - bean sprouts


Soba (そば)
Buckwheat noodles, served hot or cold. Considered a healthier option than wheat noodles.
Popular styles: - Zaru soba - cold noodles on bamboo mat with dipping sauce - Kake soba - hot noodles in broth - Tempura soba - with tempura - Kitsune soba - with sweet fried tofu - Toshikoshi soba - eaten on New Year’s Eve for longevity
Eating soba: - Slurping is encouraged and proper etiquette! - For cold soba, dip only the tips in sauce - Drink the sobayu (hot buckwheat water) at the end


Udon (うどん)
Thick wheat flour noodles, served hot or cold. Especially popular in western Japan.
Regional varieties: - Sanuki udon (Kagawa) - firm, chewy texture - Kitsune udon - with sweet fried tofu - Tempura udon - with shrimp tempura - Nabeyaki udon - in hot pot with egg and vegetables - Curry udon - in Japanese curry broth


Yakisoba (焼きそば)
Stir-fried wheat noodles with vegetables, meat, and tangy sauce. Common street food at festivals.


Somen (そうめん)
Very thin wheat noodles served cold in summer. Often served flowing in bamboo flumes at restaurants (nagashi somen).
crumbs: A Guide to Japanese Food Categories

title: A Guide to Japanese Food Categories
text:
Rice Dishes (Gohan)

Donburi (丼) - Rice Bowls
Various toppings served over a bowl of rice.
Popular varieties: - Gyudon - beef and onion simmered in sweet-savory sauce - Katsudon - breaded pork cutlet with egg - Oyakodon - chicken and egg (“parent and child”) - Tendon - tempura over rice - Unadon/Unaju - grilled eel with sweet sauce - Kaisendon - assorted raw seafood


Onigiri (おにぎり)
Rice balls wrapped in nori, often with filling. The ultimate convenience food, available everywhere.
Common fillings: - Shake - salted salmon - Umeboshi - pickled plum - Tuna mayo - tuna with mayonnaise - Okaka - bonito flakes with soy sauce - Ikura - salmon roe


Kamameshi (釜飯)
Rice cooked in individual iron pots with various ingredients.


Chazuke (茶漬け)
Rice with hot tea or dashi poured over it, often with toppings. Common as a light meal or end of meal.


Takikomi Gohan (炊き込みご飯)
Seasoned mixed rice cooked with vegetables, mushrooms, and sometimes meat or seafood.
crumbs: A Guide to Japanese Food Categories

title: A Guide to Japanese Food Categories
text:
Hot Pot Dishes (Nabe Ryori)

Sukiyaki (すき焼き)
Thinly sliced beef simmered in sweet soy sauce broth with vegetables, tofu, and noodles. Dipped in raw beaten egg before eating.


Shabu-shabu (しゃぶしゃぶ)
Thinly sliced meat and vegetables cooked by swishing in hot broth at the table. The name comes from the “swish swish” sound. Served with ponzu (citrus soy sauce) or sesame sauce.


Chanko Nabe (ちゃんこ鍋)
Protein-rich stew traditionally eaten by sumo wrestlers. Contains meat, fish, tofu, and vegetables.


Yudofu (湯豆腐)
Simple hot pot of tofu in kelp broth. Popular in Kyoto.


Oden (おでん)
Various ingredients (fish cakes, eggs, daikon, konjac) simmered in light soy-dashi broth. Popular winter street food and convenience store item.


Motsunabe (もつ鍋)
Offal (intestines) hot pot, specialty of Fukuoka. Rich, flavorful broth.
crumbs: A Guide to Japanese Food Categories

title: A Guide to Japanese Food Categories
text:
Grilled & Fried Foods

Yakitori (焼き鳥)
Grilled chicken skewers, one of Japan’s most popular drinking foods.
Common parts: - Momo - thigh meat - Negima - chicken and leek - Tsukune - chicken meatballs - Kawa - crispy skin - Tebasaki - chicken wings - Sunagimo - gizzard - Hatsu - heart - Reba - liver
Flavor options: - Shio - salt - Tare - sweet-savory sauce


Tempura (天ぷら)
Seafood and vegetables coated in light batter and deep-fried. Served with tentsuyu (dipping sauce) or salt.
Common items: - Ebi - shrimp (most popular) - Kakiage - mixed vegetable fritters - Kabocha - pumpkin - Shishito - small green peppers - Nasu - eggplant - Anago - sea eel
Tempura etiquette: - Eat immediately while hot and crispy - Dip lightly in sauce or use salt - Grated daikon in dipping sauce aids digestion


Tonkatsu (とんかつ)
Breaded and deep-fried pork cutlet. Typically served with shredded cabbage, rice, miso soup, and tonkatsu sauce.
Variations: - Rosu katsu - pork loin - Hire katsu - pork tenderloin (leaner) - Chicken katsu - breaded chicken - Menchi katsu - breaded ground meat patty


Karaage (唐揚げ)
Japanese fried chicken marinated in soy sauce, sake, and ginger. Juicy inside, crispy outside.


Kushikatsu (串カツ)
Osaka specialty - various foods breaded, skewered, and deep-fried. Never double-dip in the communal sauce!


Yakiniku (焼肉)
Japanese-style Korean BBQ where diners grill meat at the table. Popular social dining experience.


Okonomiyaki (お好み焼き)
Savory pancake with cabbage, meat/seafood, and various toppings. Name means “grilled as you like it.”
Regional styles: - Osaka style - ingredients mixed into batter - Hiroshima style - layered with noodles


Takoyaki (たこ焼き)
Osaka’s iconic street food - octopus balls in wheat batter, topped with sauce, mayo, bonito flakes, and aonori (seaweed).


Yakizakana (焼き魚)
Grilled fish - a breakfast staple. Common choices include mackerel (saba), salmon (sake), and saury (sanma).
crumbs: A Guide to Japanese Food Categories

title: A Guide to Japanese Food Categories
text:
Traditional Set Meals

Kaiseki (懐石)
The pinnacle of Japanese haute cuisine - multi-course meal emphasizing seasonality, presentation, and harmony. You’ll experience this at your welcome dinner.
Course progression: 1. Sakizuke - appetizer 2. Hassun - seasonal delicacies 3. Mukozuke - sashimi 4. Takiawase - simmered vegetables 5. Futamono - lidded dish (soup) 6. Yakimono - grilled dish 7. Su-zakana - palate cleanser 8. Hiyashi-bachi - chilled dish 9. Gohan - rice 10. Ko no mono - pickles 11. Tome-wan - final soup 12. Mizumono - dessert


Teishoku (定食)
Standard set meal at casual restaurants - includes rice, miso soup, main dish, and pickles. Excellent value and variety.
Common types: - Saba teishoku - grilled mackerel set - Tonkatsu teishoku - pork cutlet set - Tempura teishoku - tempura set - Sashimi teishoku - sashimi set


Bento (弁当)
Boxed meal with rice, protein, and vegetables. Ranges from convenience store basics to elaborate ekiben (train station specialty bentos).
Famous bento types: - Makunouchi bento - traditional theater bento - Ekiben - regional station bentos (your trip includes these!) - Kaiseki bento - upscale version
crumbs: A Guide to Japanese Food Categories

title: A Guide to Japanese Food Categories
text:
Soups

Miso Shiru (味噌汁)
Miso soup - ubiquitous at Japanese meals. Made with dashi stock and miso paste.
Common ingredients: - Tofu - Wakame - seaweed - Negi - green onions - Nameko - mushrooms - Aburaage - fried tofu


Suimono (吸い物)
Clear soup, lighter than miso soup. Often served in kaiseki meals.


Tonjiru (豚汁)
Hearty pork and vegetable miso soup.
crumbs: A Guide to Japanese Food Categories

title: A Guide to Japanese Food Categories
text:
Street Food & Snacks

Taiyaki (たい焼き)
Fish-shaped cake filled with sweet red bean paste, custard, or chocolate.


Imagawayaki / Dorayaki (今川焼き/どら焼き)
Round pancakes filled with sweet red bean paste.


Dango (団子)
Sweet rice dumplings on skewers, often with different coatings.
Types: - Mitarashi dango - with sweet soy glaze - Hanami dango - three colors (pink, white, green) - Anko dango - with sweet red bean paste


Mochi (餅)
Pounded glutinous rice cakes. Texture ranges from soft and chewy to crispy when grilled.


Senbei (煎餅)
Rice crackers in countless flavors - sweet, savory, spicy.


Melon Pan (メロンパン)
Sweet bread with crispy cookie crust, shaped like a melon.


Tamagoyaki (卵焼き)
Japanese rolled omelet, slightly sweet. Served at sushi restaurants and as breakfast side.


Korokke (コロッケ)
Japanese-style potato croquettes, often sold at butcher shops and as street food.
crumbs: A Guide to Japanese Food Categories

title: A Guide to Japanese Food Categories
text:
Sweets & Desserts (Wagashi)

Traditional Sweets

Mochi-based

Daifuku - mochi filled with sweet red bean paste
Ichigo daifuku - with whole strawberry inside
Sakura mochi - wrapped in pickled cherry leaf
Kashiwa mochi - wrapped in oak leaf



Yokan (羊羹)
Dense jellied confection made from red bean paste, agar, and sugar.


Dorayaki (どら焼き)
Two small pancakes with sweet red bean filling.


Manju (饅頭)
Steamed buns with sweet filling.


Taiyaki (鯛焼き)
Fish-shaped cake with sweet filling (mentioned in street food but also traditional).



Matcha Desserts
Green tea powder features prominently in Japanese sweets.

Matcha ice cream
Matcha parfait
Matcha roll cake
Matcha mochi
Matcha Kit-Kats (unique to Japan!)



Modern Desserts

Kakigori (かき氷)
Shaved ice dessert with various syrups and toppings. Premium versions use natural ice blocks.


Japanese Cheesecake
Ultra-fluffy, jiggly cheesecake lighter than Western versions.


Purin (プリン)
Japanese custard pudding with caramel sauce.


Parfait (パフェ)
Elaborate ice cream sundaes in tall glasses, elevated to an art form in Japan.

title: A Guide to Japanese Food Categories
text:
Beverages

Alcoholic

Sake (日本酒)
Japanese rice wine, ranging from sweet to dry, served cold or warm.
Major styles: - Junmai - pure rice sake - Honjozo - small amount of brewer’s alcohol added - Ginjo - at least 40% of rice polished away - Daiginjo - at least 50% polished (premium) - Nigori - cloudy, unfiltered - Nama - unpasteurized
Niigata sake: Your trip includes visits to sake regions. Niigata is renowned for clean, crisp sake styles due to excellent water quality and rice.
Tasting terms: - Karakuchi - dry - Amakuchi - sweet - Tanrei - light, clean - Umami - rich, full-bodied


Shochu (焼酎)
Distilled spirit stronger than sake, made from various ingredients (barley, sweet potato, rice).


Beer (ビール)
Japan has excellent beer culture.
Major brands: - Asahi Super Dry - Kirin Ichiban - Sapporo - Suntory Premium Malt’s
Craft beer scene is thriving, especially in Tokyo and Yokohama.


Japanese Whisky
World-renowned quality, aged in Japanese oak barrels.
Famous brands: - Yamazaki - Hibiki - Nikka


Chuhai (酎ハイ)
Shochu highball - shochu with carbonated water and fruit flavoring. Very popular, available in cans at convenience stores.



Non-Alcoholic

Green Tea (緑茶)
Served hot or cold, free at many restaurants.
Types: - Sencha - most common, steamed green tea - Gyokuro - premium shade-grown - Matcha - powdered green tea for ceremonies - Genmaicha - with roasted rice - Hojicha - roasted green tea (less caffeine) - Mugicha - roasted barley tea (caffeine-free)


Japanese Coffee
Japan takes coffee seriously with countless specialty cafes and kissaten (traditional coffee shops).
Styles: - Pour-over - hand-dripped, common method - Siphon coffee - vacuum pot brewing - Canned coffee - hot and cold, from vending machines


Ramune (ラムネ)
Carbonated soft drink in distinctive glass bottle with marble stopper.


Calpis (カルピス)
Sweet, milky, non-carbonated soft drink with unique tangy flavor.


Japanese Vending Machines
Ubiquitous! Offer hot and cold drinks, including: - Green tea - Coffee (hot and cold) - Sports drinks (Pocari Sweat, Aquarius) - Juices - Calpis - Beer (some machines)

title: A Guide to Japanese Food Categories
text:
Food Etiquette & Customs

General Dining Etiquette
Do: - Say “Itadakimasu” (いただきます) before eating - “I humbly receive” - Say “Gochisousama deshita” (ごちそうさまでした) after eating - “Thank you for the meal” - Slurp noodles - shows appreciation and cools noodles - Pick up bowls of rice and miso soup to eat - Use both hands when passing dishes or receiving items - Wait for “kampai” (cheers) before drinking in group settings - Rest chopsticks on the holder (hashioki) when not using
Don’t: - Stick chopsticks upright in rice (funeral ritual) - Pass food chopstick-to-chopstick (funeral ritual) - Rub disposable chopsticks together (suggests poor quality) - Point with chopsticks - Stab food with chopsticks - Mix wasabi into soy sauce (at high-end sushi places) - Pour your own drink (pour for others, they’ll pour yours) - Tip (considered rude)


Sushi-Specific Etiquette

Eat nigiri in one bite
Dip fish side in soy sauce, not rice
Use hands or chopsticks (both acceptable)
Trust the chef at omakase restaurants
Eat pieces in order presented



Restaurant Etiquette

Say “Sumimasen” (すみません) to call staff - “Excuse me”
Don’t leave tips - included in service
Many restaurants have tablet ordering or call buttons
Some restaurants have plastic food displays - point if needed
Oshibori (hot towels) are for hands only
Remove shoes at traditional restaurants with tatami
Smoke only in designated smoking areas/restaurants



Kaiseki Etiquette

Courses are served in specific order - eat in order presented
Use all provided utensils (sometimes hands)
Appreciate presentation before eating
Finish everything - waste is disrespectful
Pace yourself - many courses ahead!



Onsen Etiquette (relevant to your Fuji stay)
Not food-related but important: - Wash thoroughly before entering bath - No swimsuits in onsen - Small towel for modesty, don’t put in water - Tattoos may not be allowed (check with hotel)

title: A Guide to Japanese Food Categories
text:
Convenience Store Foods

Japanese convenience stores (konbini) are treasure troves:
7-Eleven (セブンイレブン) Lawson (ローソン) Family Mart (ファミリーマート)
Must-try items: - Onigiri - rice balls (look for salmon, tuna mayo, mentaiko) - Sandwiches - egg salad, katsu sando (pork cutlet) - Oden - simmered items in broth by the register - Nikuman - steamed pork buns (hot case) - Instant ramen - cup noodles with hot water available - Fried chicken - freshly fried at register - Desserts - Japanese cheesecake, pudding, mochi - Drinks - every beverage imaginable - Seasonal items - constantly rotating limited editions
Services: - ATMs (international cards accepted) - Copy/print services - Luggage shipping - Bill payment - Concert ticket purchases - Bathroom (ask “toire wa doko desu ka?”)

title: A Guide to Japanese Food Categories
text:
Dietary Restrictions

Vegetarian/Vegan
Japanese cuisine traditionally includes fish stock (dashi) in many dishes, making strict vegetarian/vegan dining challenging.
Options: - Shojin ryori - Buddhist temple cuisine (completely vegetarian) - Vegetable tempura - Inari sushi - sushi rice in fried tofu - Vegetable ramen - specify “yasai ramen” - Edamame - soybeans - Nasu dengaku - grilled eggplant with miso
Learn these phrases: - “Bejitarian desu” (ベジタリアンです) - “I’m vegetarian” - “Niku to sakana ga taberaremasen” (肉と魚が食べられません) - “I can’t eat meat and fish” - “Dashi mo dame desu” (だしもダメです) - “No fish stock either”


Gluten-Free
Difficult as soy sauce contains wheat. Look for: - Sashimi (check the soy sauce) - Yakitori with salt (shio) - Rice dishes - Tamari - gluten-free soy sauce (rare but exists)
Phrase: - “Komugi arerugi desu” (小麦アレルギーです) - “I have a wheat allergy”


Food Allergies
Japan takes allergies seriously. Restaurants often have allergy charts.
Common allergens: - Tamago - egg - Gyunyu - milk - Komugi - wheat - Soba - buckwheat - Ebi - shrimp/prawn - Kani - crab
Phrase: - “___ arerugi desu” - “I’m allergic to ___”

Tips for Food Adventurers

General Tips

Plastic food displays - Windows show realistic models, point if needed
Photo menus - Many restaurants have picture menus
Ticket machines - Some restaurants use vending machines for ordering

Money-Saving Tips

Convenience store meals are cheap and good
Evening supermarket discounts (7-9 PM)
Lunch sets at restaurants (cheaper than dinner)
Free water at restaurants
Standing sushi bars (less expensive)
100-yen shops for snacks and drinks
Onigiri for breakfast or snacks

title: A Guide to Japanese Food Categories
text:
Food Safety & Practical Notes

Tap water is safe throughout Japan
Food safety standards are exceptional
Raw fish is extremely safe - among world’s highest quality
Restrooms - Look for “W.C.” or “Toilet” signs, or in convenience stores
Allergies - Taken seriously; many menus list allergens
Trash bins - Rare on streets; carry trash until you find one
Smoking - Many restaurants still allow smoking; look for non-smoking (禁煙 - kin’en)
Credit cards - Not universal; carry cash especially for small restaurants

title: A Guide to Japanese Food Categories
text:
Cultural Context

Why Japanese Food Tastes Different
Umami - The fifth taste (alongside sweet, sour, salty, bitter). Japanese cuisine pioneered understanding of umami through dashi, soy sauce, and miso.
Five principles: 1. Shun (旬) - Seasonality 2. Quality ingredients - Minimal processing 3. Balance - Five colors, five tastes, five cooking methods 4. Presentation - Food is visual art 5. Harmony - With nature and seasons


The Role of Rice
Rice (gohan) is so central that the word also means “meal.” Historically, side dishes were considered accompaniments to rice, not vice versa.


Fish Culture
Island nation surrounded by seafood = incredibly fresh fish and advanced preservation techniques (fermentation, drying, salting).


Fermentation
Japanese cuisine excels in fermentation: - Soy sauce - Miso - Sake - Natto - Tsukemono (pickles)

title: Hiroshima
text:
On this date and time, the United States dropped the first atomic bomb used in warfare on Hiroshima. The bomb, called “Little Boy,” detonated about 600 meters above the city center. In an instant:

The city center was obliterated
An estimated 70,000-80,000 people died immediately
By the end of 1945, the death toll reached approximately 140,000
Tens of thousands more died in subsequent years from radiation effects and injuries
90% of the city was destroyed

Three days later, a second bomb was dropped on Nagasaki. Japan surrendered on August 15, 1945, ending World War II.



Military significance: - Major military headquarters - Important port and industrial center - Troop staging area - Had been largely untouched by previous bombing campaigns (to assess the bomb’s full impact)
Civilian reality: - The city was home to approximately 350,000 people - Many victims were civilians, including children - The hypocenter was near the densely populated city center.



The atomic bombings remain controversial:
Arguments that it was necessary: - Ended the war quickly, preventing a costly invasion of Japan - Saved American (and potentially Japanese) lives that would have been lost in continued fighting - Japan’s military leadership showed no signs of surrendering.
Arguments against: - Civilians, not just military targets, bore the brunt - Japan was already devastated and near surrender - The radiation effects were not fully understood - Established a terrifying precedent for nuclear warfare.
Today’s consensus: Most historians agree it was a pivotal moment in human history that changed warfare forever, regardless of one’s position on its necessity.

title: Hiroshima
section: Your Visit: Making the Most of 2 Hours
text:
Your Visit: Making the Most of 2 Hours

The Challenge
Two hours is tight, but it’s possible to have a meaningful experience if you’re focused. The three sites are all in the same area (Peace Memorial Park), so no travel time between them.


Recommended Route & Timing
Total time available: 2 hours

Option A: Museum Focus (Recommended)

Atomic Bomb Dome (outside viewing): 15 minutes
Peace Memorial Museum: 75 minutes
Peace Memorial Park monuments: 30 minutes



Option B: Balanced Approach

Atomic Bomb Dome: 20 minutes
Peace Memorial Museum: 60 minutes (abbreviated visit)
Peace Memorial Park monuments: 40 minutes

My recommendation: Option A. The museum provides essential context that makes everything else more meaningful.

title: Hiroshima
text:
The Three Sites

1. Atomic Bomb Dome (Genbaku Dome)
What It Is: The skeletal ruins of the former Hiroshima Prefectural Industrial Promotion Hall. It was one of the few structures left standing near the hypocenter because the bomb detonated almost directly overhead, and the blast came downward rather than laterally.
What to See: - The preserved ruins (viewed from outside) - Information plaques explaining its significance - The hypocenter marker nearby - View across the river to Peace Memorial Park
Time Needed: 15-20 minutes
What to Know: - You cannot go inside - it’s preserved as-is and fenced off - It’s a UNESCO World Heritage Site - The dome is a powerful symbol of the bombing—hauntingly beautiful and tragic - Best viewed from the bank across the river for full perspective
Photography: - Photography is allowed and expected - Be respectful—this is essentially a grave site - Early morning or late afternoon light is best for photos



2. Peace Memorial Museum
What It Is: The main museum documenting the atomic bombing, its effects, and the path to peace. Renovated and reopened in 2019 with updated exhibits.
What to Expect:
East Building (Main Exhibition): - Personal belongings of victims (watches stopped at 8:15, burned clothing, lunch boxes) - Survivor testimonies (video and written) - Photographs of the aftermath - Medical effects of radiation - Individual stories of victims (including children)
Main Building: - Lead-up to the bombing - The bomb’s development - Aftermath and reconstruction - Nuclear weapons today - Hiroshima’s role in the peace movement
Time Needed: - Minimum: 60 minutes (abbreviated, hitting main exhibits) - Ideal: 90-120 minutes (thorough viewing) - With only 2 hours total, plan for 60-75 minutes here
Emotional Impact: - This is heavy. The personal artifacts are particularly affecting - Children’s items are especially difficult to see - The photographs of victims are graphic and disturbing - Survivor testimonies are heartbreaking - Many visitors leave in tears—this is normal and expected
Practical Info: - Admission: ¥200 (about $1.50) - minimal fee - Audio guide: Available in English (recommended) - English signage: Excellent—all major exhibits have English - Photography: Generally not allowed inside exhibits (check current policy) - Facilities: Lockers, restrooms, quiet reflection spaces - Accessibility: Fully wheelchair accessible
Tips: - Start with the orientation film if available (provides context) - Focus on the East Building first (most impactful) - Don’t rush—let yourself absorb what you’re seeing - Take breaks if needed (there are rest areas) - The museum shop has books and information if you want to learn more



3. Peace Memorial Park
What It Is: A large park built on the site of the bomb’s hypocenter, now dedicated to peace. Contains numerous monuments and memorials.
Key Monuments (in order of importance for a short visit):

Memorial Cenotaph

Arch-shaped monument containing names of all known victims
Flame of Peace burns in front (will burn until all nuclear weapons are eliminated)
Perfectly aligned view through to the Atomic Bomb Dome
This alignment is intentional and powerful

Time: 5 minutes


Children’s Peace Monument

Inspired by Sadako Sasaki, who died of leukemia from radiation exposure at age 12
She folded 1,000 paper cranes hoping to recover
Now, millions of paper cranes from around the world are displayed here
Particularly moving for many visitors

Time: 5-10 minutes


Memorial Mound

Contains ashes of 70,000 unidentified victims
Simple but profound

Time: 5 minutes


Peace Bell

Visitors can ring it (a prayer for peace)
Map of world without borders cast on the bell

Time: 5 minutes


Korean Victims Memorial

Often overlooked but important
About 10% of victims were Korean (forced laborers and residents)

Time: 5 minutes if you have time
Park Overall: - Beautiful, peaceful, well-maintained - Cherry blossoms in spring - Good place for reflection after the museum - Riverside walk is pleasant
Time Needed: 20-40 minutes depending on how many monuments you visit

title: Hiroshima
text:
Your 2-Hour Itinerary

Arrival

Arrive at Peace Memorial Park (likely via streetcar or bus from Hiroshima Station)
Orient yourself—get a park map if available



Hour 1: Understanding Through Context
0:00-0:15 - Atomic Bomb Dome - Walk to the Dome first - View from outside, take in the significance - Read information plaques - This provides visual context for what you’ll learn in the museum
0:15-1:30 - Peace Memorial Museum - 75 minutes in the museum - Start with East Building (personal stories and artifacts) - Main Building if time allows - Don’t rush the experience


Hour 2: Reflection and Remembrance
1:30-1:40 - Memorial Cenotaph & Peace Flame - Stand at the cenotaph - Take in the view through to the Dome - Moment of reflection
1:40-1:50 - Children’s Peace Monument - See the paper cranes - Read about Sadako’s story - Very moving
1:50-2:00 - Final walk or chosen monument - If you have specific interest (Korean memorial, Peace Bell, etc.) - Or simply walk through the park - Let the experience settle

title: Hiroshima
text:
What to Expect Emotionally

This Will Be Heavy
There’s no way around it. Hiroshima’s Peace Memorial is one of the most emotionally challenging historical sites you can visit. Here’s what to expect:
In the museum: - Personal artifacts that make the abstract horror concrete - Photographs that are difficult to look at - Stories of real people, especially children - The scale of suffering is overwhelming
Common reactions: - Crying (very common—don’t be embarrassed) - Feeling numb or overwhelmed - Need for silence and processing time - Questioning humanity’s capacity for such destruction - Gratitude that nuclear weapons haven’t been used since
Why it’s important despite being difficult: - Bearing witness to what happened - Understanding the human cost of nuclear warfare - Honoring the victims by learning their stories - Reinforcing commitment to peace


Cultural Context
Hiroshima’s message is not about blame—it’s about peace.
The museum and memorials are remarkably non-accusatory. The focus is on: - What happened to the people of Hiroshima - The effects of nuclear weapons - The need to abolish nuclear weapons - Creating a peaceful world
You will not encounter: - Anti-American sentiment (in general) - Blame placed on visitors - Political messaging about WWII causes
As an American visitor: - You may feel complex emotions - This is understandable and okay - The goal is learning and reflection, not guilt - Japanese visitors experience it just as intensely

title: Hiroshima
text:
Practical Information

Getting There
From Hiroshima Station: - Streetcar (tram): Lines 2 or 6 to “Genbaku Dome-mae” stop (15-20 min, ¥220) - Bus: Various routes to Peace Memorial Park - Taxi: 10 minutes, about ¥1,000 - Recommendation: Take the streetcar (it’s part of the Hiroshima experience)
Location: Peace Memorial Park is on an island between two rivers in downtown Hiroshima.


Timing Your Visit
Best times: - Early morning (museum opens 8:30am) - fewer crowds - Weekdays - less busy than weekends - Not August 6 - Anniversary of bombing is extremely crowded
Season considerations: - Spring: Cherry blossoms make park beautiful but very crowded - Summer: Hot and humid, but August 6 ceremony is significant - Fall: Pleasant weather, fewer tourists - Winter: Cold but least crowded


Facilities
In/Near the Park: - Restrooms throughout park - Vending machines - Benches for rest - Museum has lockers, gift shop, cafe
Nearby: - Restaurants along Peace Boulevard - Convenience stores - Starbucks and other cafes (if you need to decompress)


What to Bring
Essential: - Passport (for museum discount, if applicable) - Tissues (you may cry, and it’s dusty in museum) - Water bottle (especially in summer) - Comfortable shoes (you’ll be walking and standing)
Optional: - Camera (but be respectful about what you photograph) - Notebook (for reflection) - Small donation for children’s peace monument crane collection


What NOT to Bring

Large bags (use museum lockers)
Food/drinks into museum
Loud or disrespectful attitude

title: Hiroshima
text:
After Your Visit

Processing the Experience
Give yourself transition time: - Don’t rush immediately to something lighthearted - Maybe walk along the river - Sit in the park for a few moments - Let the experience settle before moving on
Talk about it: - With your travel companion - In your journal - With your Wisconsin Quilt Museum tour group - Processing helps integrate the experience
Learn more: - The museum gift shop has excellent books - “Hiroshima” by John Hersey (classic journalism) - “Barefoot Gen” manga by Keiji Nakazawa (survivor’s story)


Connecting to Today
Hiroshima’s message: The city has rebuilt itself as a symbol of peace and resilience. Walking through modern, thriving Hiroshima after visiting the memorial sites is itself a powerful statement about humanity’s ability to rebuild and choose peace.
Nuclear weapons today: The museum includes information about current nuclear stockpiles. As of 2024, there are still about 12,500 nuclear weapons in the world. Hiroshima’s message of abolition remains urgent and relevant.

title: Hiroshima
text:
Cultural Sensitivity

Do’s:

Be respectful and quiet in the museum and at monuments
Take your time - don’t rush through
Read the stories - honor the victims by learning about them
Reflect genuinely on what you’re seeing
Support the message of peace and nuclear abolition
Donate to the paper crane collection if moved to do so



Don’ts:

Don’t take selfies at the Dome or monuments (extremely disrespectful)
Don’t talk loudly or treat it as just another tourist site
Don’t debate WWII politics at the memorial sites
Don’t rush - this deserves your attention
Don’t bring young children unless you’re prepared to explain difficult concepts



Photography Guidelines:

Dome and park: Yes, but be tasteful
Museum exhibits: Generally no (check current policy)
Other visitors: Be mindful of people having emotional moments
Remember: This is essentially a grave site and memorial

title: Hiroshima
section: Why This Matters for Your Trip
text:
Why This Matters for Your Trip

Historical Understanding
Visiting Hiroshima helps complete the picture of 20th-century East Asian history:

Korea: Colonized by Japan, affected by WWII’s end, Korean War
Japan: Aggressor in WWII, but also victim of atomic bombs, rebuilt as pacifist nation
The legacy: How these events shaped modern Korea and Japan



The Peace Connection
Your trip includes: - Korean DMZ (ongoing division from war) - Japanese textile/art tour (creativity and culture) - Hiroshima (ultimate cost of war)
Together, these experiences show both the destruction humans are capable of and our capacity to create, rebuild, and choose peace.


Personal Impact
This visit will likely stay with you. Many people cite Hiroshima as one of the most impactful experiences of their lives—not because it’s pleasant, but because it’s profound.

title: Hiroshima
text:
Practical Checklist
Before you go: - [ ] Check museum hours (usually 8:30am-6pm, but varies by season) - [ ] Download offline map of Peace Memorial Park - [ ] Read brief history of atomic bombing - [ ] Prepare emotionally for difficult content - [ ] Check weather (bring umbrella if needed)
At the site: - [ ] Start at Atomic Bomb Dome for orientation - [ ] Spend majority of time in museum - [ ] Visit Children’s Peace Monument - [ ] Stand at Memorial Cenotaph - [ ] Take time to reflect before leaving
After: - [ ] Give yourself decompression time - [ ] Consider supporting peace organizations if moved - [ ] Share what you learned (respectfully) - [ ] Let the experience inform how you think about peace and conflict

title: Hiroshima
text:
Final Thoughts
Two hours is short for Hiroshima, but if you focus and are intentional, you can have a meaningful experience. The key is:

Prioritize the museum - this is where understanding happens
Don’t try to see everything - choose a few key monuments
Be present - put your phone away, really engage with what you’re seeing
Allow yourself to feel - this is supposed to be moving
Take the message forward - Hiroshima’s story is about creating peace, not dwelling on tragedy

Hiroshima is not easy, but it’s important. In a world that still has nuclear weapons, bearing witness to what they do is a responsibility we all share.

“The spirit of Hiroshima—that enduring, endlessly patient effort to change minds and win hearts—lives on in the Peace Memorial Park and in the people of this remarkable city.” - President Barack Obama, 2016

title: Hiroshima Optional Excursion
text:
Important Information

We are all still seeing Himeji Castle. When the main group heads to lunch after the Castle visit, we will head to the train station to catch our train to Hiroshima.
We will be getting Bento box lunches at the train station (I’ll give you yen).
Our itinerary for the day is pretty tight in order to see the Hiroshima Peace Memorial Museum, Peace Park, and Hiroshima Castle. We’ll all agree on times to be back at our meeting places. Please be sure to be on time. See below for details.
Bullet trains do not wait very long. It is crucial we have our belongings/trash from lunch gathered before our stop.
We’ll be sharing taxis from the train station to our various stops. I’ll provide yen as we’ll need to split into a few smaller groups (all going to the same places though!).
Dinner is on your own. You may either pick up another Bento box at the train station or wait until you get back to Kyoto. The train ride is approximately 1.5 hours. Unfortunately, we won’t have time to eat in Hiroshima.
Our day officially ends back at the hotel in Kyoto.

title: Hiroshima Optional Excursion
text:
Itinerary
12:46 - Departing Himeji Station on bullet train
13:42 - Arriving in Hiroshima

Take a taxi (10 min) to the Memorial Museum
Group will divide into 2 or 3 taxis (I’ll give you yen for ride)

14:00-15:30 - Peace Memorial Museum

Ticket entry time is 14:00

15:30-16:00 - Peace Park & Atomic Bomb Dome

10 min walk from the museum

16:30-17:30 - Hiroshima Castle

Take a taxi (10 min) from Peace Park
Group will again divide into 2 or 3 taxis (I’ll give you yen for ride)

17:45 - Depart Hiroshima Castle (latest)

Take a taxi (10 min) back to Hiroshima Station

18:29 - Departing Hiroshima Station on bullet train
20:13 - Arriving in Kyoto

Taxi back to hotel (group will divide into 2 or 3 taxis & I’ll give you yen for ride)

title: Japanese Stationary
text:
Must-Visit Shops

Traveler’s Factory (Nakameguro)
The Holy Grail for Traveler’s Notebook Fans
Location: Nakameguro, Tokyo
Nearest Station: Nakameguro Station (Hibiya Line)
Address: 〒153-0051 Tokyo, Meguro City, Kamimeguro, 3 Chome−13−10
From Hotel East 21: - Toyocho Station (Tozai Line) → Otemachi (transfer) → Hibiya Line to Nakameguro - Time: 35-40 minutes - Alternative: Toyocho → Nihombashi (transfer) → Hibiya Line to Nakameguro - Distance: About 12km
What Makes It Special: - Official flagship store of Traveler’s Company - Original Traveler’s Notebook leather covers in all sizes - Exclusive limited editions and collaborations - Every refill, accessory, and customization option - Vintage brass accessories - Factory Exclusive items you can’t get anywhere else - Watch craftspeople work on notebooks - Small cafe upstairs (coffee and atmosphere)
What to Buy: - Traveler’s Notebook leather covers (passport, regular, A5 sizes) - Refills in every style (blank, lined, grid, kraft, watercolor) - Brass clips, rulers, pens - Stickers, stamps, decorative items - Limited edition seasonal items - Customization services
Tips: - Go early or late to avoid crowds - Allow 1-2 hours to browse properly - Bring extra luggage space - Staff speak English - Very Instagrammable location
Hours: Usually 12pm-8pm (check current hours)
Website: travelers-factory.com



Itoya (Ginza)
12 Floors of Stationery Heaven
Location: Ginza, Tokyo
Nearest Station: Ginza Station (multiple lines)
Address: 2-7-15 Ginza, Chuo-ku, Tokyo
From Hotel East 21: - Toyocho Station (Tozai Line) → Direct to Nihombashi (3 stops) → Walk 10 min OR transfer to Ginza Line (1 stop to Ginza) - Time: 20-25 minutes total - Easiest route: Toyocho → Otemachi (2 stops) → Transfer to any line to Ginza (2 stops) - Distance: About 8km - This is one of your closest major shops!
What Makes It Special: - Iconic Tokyo stationery store since 1904 - 12 floors dedicated to different stationery categories - Incredible mechanical pencil selection - High-end and everyday items - Beautiful building, each floor color-coded - Main store + K.Itoya annex across the street
Floor Guide: - B1F: Greeting cards, gift wrap - 1F: Fountain pens, luxury writing instruments - 2F: Japanese paper products, washi - 3F: Mechanical pencils, ballpoint pens, pencils - 4F: Notebooks, journals, planners - 5F: Business supplies, desk accessories - 6F-11F: Various specialized items - 12F: Red Cafe with Ginza views
What to Buy: - Premium mechanical pencils (Pentel, Pilot, Uni, Zebra, Rotring) - Midori MD Notebooks - Hobonichi planners and notebooks - Washi tape and stickers - High-quality fountain pens - Unique desk accessories
Tips: - Start at top and work down, or focus on floors 3-4 for pencils/notebooks - Check out K.Itoya annex too (more lifestyle/design items) - Tax-free shopping available with passport - Can easily spend 2-3 hours here
Hours: 10am-8pm (Mon-Sat), 10am-7pm (Sun)
Website: www.ito-ya.co.jp



Tokyu Hands (Multiple Locations)
Department Store Meets DIY Haven
Best Location: Shibuya (8 floors)
Other Locations: Shinjuku, Ikebukuro, many more
From Hotel East 21 to Shibuya: - Toyocho Station (Tozai Line) → Otemachi → Transfer to Hanzomon Line → Shibuya - Time: 35-40 minutes - Alternative: Via Nihombashi with transfers - Distance: About 15km
From Hotel East 21 to Shinjuku: - Nishi-ojima Station (Toei Shinjuku Line) → Direct to Shinjuku-sanchome - Time: 25-30 minutes (this is your EASIEST route!) - Distance: About 12km - Shinjuku Tokyu Hands is most convenient for you!
What Makes It Special: - Huge department store with amazing stationery floor - Mid-range prices - Great variety without overwhelming - Easy to navigate - Multiple locations across Tokyo
What to Buy: - Good selection of mechanical pencils (all major brands) - Notebooks and planners - Masking tape and craft supplies - Unique Japanese organizational tools - DIY and craft materials - Practical everyday stationery
Tips: - Shibuya location is largest and most comprehensive - Stationery usually on 5th or 6th floor - Less overwhelming than some specialty shops - Good for “I need to pick up various items” shopping
Hours: Vary by location, typically 10am-9pm



Loft (Multiple Locations)
Trendy Lifestyle & Stationery
Best Locations: Shibuya, Ginza, Ikebukuro
Flagship: Shibuya Loft (7 floors)
From Hotel East 21: - To Ginza Loft: Same as Itoya above (20-25 minutes) - CLOSEST - To Shibuya Loft: 35-40 minutes via Tozai Line → Otemachi → Hanzomon Line - To Ikebukuro: Toyocho → Otemachi → Marunouchi Line to Ikebukuro (30 min) - Recommendation: Visit Ginza Loft - it’s your closest option!
What Makes It Special: - Curated selection of trendy stationery - Japanese and imported brands - Design-forward products - Good prices - Hip, young vibe
What to Buy: - Midori products (MD notebooks, Traveler’s items) - Cute Japanese character stationery - Innovative desk tools - Imported stationery brands - Washi tape paradise - Planners and organizers
Tips: - Stationery usually takes up 1-2 full floors - Great for gifts - Less “serious” than Itoya, more fun/colorful - Tax-free shopping available
Hours: Typically 10am-9pm



Kakimori (Kuramae)
Custom Notebooks & Artisan Ink
Location: Kuramae, Tokyo (trendy artisan neighborhood)
Nearest Station: Kuramae Station (Asakusa or Oedo Line)
From Hotel East 21: - Toyocho Station (Tozai Line) → Nihombashi → Transfer to Asakusa Line → Kuramae - Time: 25-30 minutes - Alternative: Tozai Line → Monzen-nakacho → Oedo Line → Kuramae
- Distance: About 6km - This is relatively close to your hotel!
What Makes It Special: - Custom notebook service - choose paper, binding, cover - Custom ink blending (create your own fountain pen ink color!) - Artisan, handcrafted focus - Beautiful minimal aesthetic - Small but perfectly curated
What to Buy: - Custom-made notebooks (takes 30-60 minutes) - Blended custom ink - Writing instruments - Limited selection but highest quality - Unique gifts
Tips: - Plan for time if doing custom notebook - Great for serious stationery enthusiasts - Combine with exploring Kuramae neighborhood (cafes, vintage shops) - English spoken
Hours: 11am-7pm (closed Mondays)
Website: kakimori.com



Maruzen (Multiple Locations)
Books + Premium Stationery
Best Location: Maruzen Marunouchi (near Tokyo Station)
From Hotel East 21: - Toyocho Station (Tozai Line) → Direct to Otemachi (2 stops, 5 min) → Walk 10 min to Marunouchi - Time: 15-20 minutes total - Distance: About 6km - This is your CLOSEST major shop - super convenient! - Can also get off at Tokyo Station (one more stop) and walk
What Makes It Special: - Traditional bookstore with excellent stationery section - High-quality selection - Less touristy than Itoya - Connected to Japanese literary culture - Elegant atmosphere
What to Buy: - Quality mechanical pencils - Fountain pens - Traditional Japanese paper products - Business journals and planners - Gift-quality items
Tips: - Great if you also want English books about Japan - More refined, less overwhelming - Good for serious writing enthusiasts



Sekaido (Shinjuku)
Art Supplies + Stationery
Location: Shinjuku
Nearest Station: Shinjuku Station
From Hotel East 21: - Nishi-ojima Station (Toei Shinjuku Line) → Direct to Shinjuku-sanchome (no transfers!) - Time: 25-30 minutes - Distance: About 12km - Easy direct route - no transfers needed!
What Makes It Special: - Art supplies meet stationery - 5 floors - Great prices - Less touristy - Locals’ favorite
What to Buy: - Mechanical pencils (including drafting pencils) - Sketch books and art notebooks - Technical drawing supplies - Manga supplies - Affordable quality items
Tips: - Good for artists and designers - More “working supplies” than “precious stationery” - Solid everyday prices

title: Japanese Stationary
text:
Specialty Items Guide

Mechanical Pencils
Where to Find Best Selection: 1. Itoya (Floor 3) - Largest variety, all price points 2. Tokyu Hands - Good mid-range selection 3. Sekaido - Technical/drafting pencils
Japanese Brands to Look For: - Pentel: GraphGear 500/1000, Orenz Nero (unbreakable lead) - Pilot: S20, S10, S5, S3 - Uni (Mitsubishi): Kuru Toga (rotating lead mechanism), Shift - Zebra: DelGuard (lead break protection) - Platinum: MSD series, Pro-Use 171 - Ohto: Conception, Promecha - Tombow: Mono Graph series
Price Ranges: - Budget: ¥300-800 ($2-6) - Mid-range: ¥800-3,000 ($6-20) - Premium: ¥3,000-10,000+ ($20-70+)
Unique Japanese Features: - Rotating lead mechanisms (Kuru Toga) - Lead break prevention systems - Ultra-precise lead grades (2H to 4B) - 0.2mm ultra-fine leads - Ergonomic grip designs



Traveler’s Notebooks
Where to Buy: 1. Traveler’s Factory - THE place, exclusive items 2. Itoya - Good selection, regular line 3. Loft - Decent selection, accessories 4. Tokyu Hands - Basic selection
What to Get: - Leather Covers: Passport, Regular (A5), or A4 sizes - Refills: Choose your paper style (grid, lined, blank, kraft, watercolor) - Brass Accessories: Clips, pen holders, rulers - Charms and Customization: Make it yours
Traveler’s Factory Exclusives: - Limited edition colors - Special collaborations - Factory-stamping service - Unique refills



Other Japanese Stationery to Explore
Notebooks: - Midori MD Notebook - Minimal design, excellent paper - Hobonichi Techo - Cult-favorite planner - Life Noble Note - Premium writing paper - Apica Premium CD - Smooth writing experience
Pens: - Pilot Juice - Smooth gel pens - Uni-ball Signo - Waterproof, fade-proof - Zebra Sarasa - Quick-dry gel ink - Pentel Energel - Fast-drying
Unique Items: - Washi Tape (mt brand is iconic) - Sticky Notes (creative shapes and designs) - Correction Tape (Japanese engineering at its finest) - Page Flags (aesthetic and functional) - Desk Organizers (minimal, beautiful)

title: Japanese Stationary
text:
Shopping Strategy

From Hotel East 21: Proximity Guide
Closest & Easiest (15-25 minutes): 1. Maruzen Marunouchi - 15-20 min via Tozai Line direct 2. Itoya Ginza - 20-25 min via Tozai Line → Otemachi 3. Ginza Loft - 20-25 min (same route as Itoya) 4. Kakimori Kuramae - 25-30 min via Tozai/Asakusa Lines
Medium Distance (25-35 minutes): 5. Shinjuku (Tokyu Hands, Sekaido) - 25-30 min via Toei Shinjuku Line direct (no transfers!)
Farther but Worth It (35-40 minutes): 6. Traveler’s Factory Nakameguro - 35-40 min (requires transfer) 7. Shibuya (Tokyu Hands, Loft) - 35-40 min (requires transfer)

title: East Japan Railway Ekiben Grand Prix
text:
Ekiben Grand Prix 2025



From the Website:
In Japan, it is a cherished culture to enjoy a bento meal while traveling on long-distance trains.
The specially prepared lunch boxes sold at train stations for this purpose are called ekiben. Each ekiben showcases the local flavors of its region, featuring specialties, seasonal ingredients, and creative recipes designed to taste great on the train. They are also beautifully wrapped in unique, colorful paper called “kakegami,” making them delightful to look at. To promote this culinary tradition, JR East hosts the Ekiben Grand Prix campaign. Join this campaign to discover great ekiben. Taking part is easy, just by clicking “Like” on the ekiben that looks the most tempting to you. With 63 unique ekiben lunch boxes packed with flavors from across Japan, find your favorite ekiben!



This year marks the 140th anniversary of ekiben. It is said that the very first ekiben was sold in 1885, containing nothing more than two rice balls and a few pickles. Since then, ekiben has transformed into a showcase of Japan’s regional flavors, now packed with savory meats and fresh seafood.



Ekiben lunch boxes are carefully crafted to be enjoyed on the train, even hours after they are made.

* Seasoned to taste delicious even when eaten cold.
* Packed neatly so the presentation stays beautiful, despite the train’s motion.
* Thoughtfully prepared with subtle aromas, so that they will not disturb the people sitting nearby - reflecting Japan’s culture of consideration.

title: Japanese Denim
text:
Sizing Guide

Important: Japanese Sizing
Japanese sizes run 1-2 sizes smaller than US/EU: - US 32 = Japan 34 or 36 - US 34 = Japan 36 or 38 - Measure your best-fitting jeans and compare - ALWAYS try on before buying


Fit Styles
Regular/Vintage: - Higher rise - Roomier thigh - Straight or slight taper - Authentic vintage proportions
Slim/Modern: - Lower rise - Tapered leg - Contemporary fit - More fashion-forward
Relaxed: - Comfortable fit - Room in seat and thigh - Workwear heritage


Key Measurements
Bring these measurements (in inches or cm): - Waist (where you wear pants) - Inseam (length you need) - Thigh circumference - Leg opening
Pro tip: Most shops have measuring tapes and will help you find the right size.

What to Expect

The Experience

Buying Process

title: Japanese Denim
text:
Care & Breaking In

What Staff Will Tell You
First Wear: - Don’t wash for at least 6 months (or longer) - The longer you wait, the better the fades - Some people wait a year or more
Breaking In: - Will be stiff at first - Fabric will soften and conform to your body - Be patient through the first few weeks
First Wash: - Turn inside out - Cold water - No detergent (or very mild) - Hang dry - Never machine dry
Philosophy: - The jeans are an investment - They tell your story through fading - The “best” jeans are worn hard and washed little - Embrace the process

title: Japanese Denim
text:
Tips for Success

Before You Go
Research: - Look at fade photos online (search “[brand name] fade”) - Understand different denim weights (12oz-25oz) - Know what style you want (vintage, modern, workwear)
Bring: - Passport (for tax-free) - Measurements of your best-fitting jeans - Extra cash - Patience for trying things on


At the Shop
Do: - Try on multiple sizes - Ask about fade characteristics - Ask to see fade examples - Take your time - Ask questions about care
Don’t: - Rush the decision - Buy based only on tagged size (Japanese sizing varies) - Be afraid to go heavier weight than expected - Forget about shrinkage (raw denim shrinks 1-2 inches in waist/length)


Language

Most shops have English-speaking staff
Denim terms are often universal
Point to examples if needed
Staff are used to international customers

title: Japanese Denim
text:
Why Japanese Denim?

What Makes It Special
Craftsmanship: - Made on vintage shuttle looms - Attention to every detail - Artisan approach to production - Small-batch manufacturing
Quality: - Premium long-staple cotton - Natural indigo dyeing (some brands) - Selvedge construction - Will last decades
Fading: - Creates unique wear patterns - Tells your story - Each pair becomes one-of-a-kind - “Earned” patina
Heritage: - Japanese companies perfected American denim - Combined US heritage with Japanese precision - Now considered the world’s best denim

title: Japanese Denim

Final Recommendations

If You Can Only Visit ONE Shop:

If You Want Easy Convenience:

If You Want Premium Experience:

If You’re Serious About Denim:
Visit 2-3 shops - Hinoya for variety, Blue in Green for premium, maybe Americanaya or Bears for different perspective

Bottom Line: Japanese denim is an investment in quality and craftsmanship. Take your time, try things on, ask questions, and enjoy the experience. Your hotel location gives you good access to the best shops, with Ueno’s Hinoya being your top priority and Shinjuku’s Okura being your most convenient option.
Wear them hard, wash them rarely, and watch them become your favorite jeans.

Location: Ginza
