Specialty: High-end artisan chopsticks from across Japan
The premier chopstick destination in Tokyo, Ginza Natsuno has been a chopstick specialty store with an incredible selection ranging from elegant everyday pairs to museum-quality artisan pieces. The staff is knowledgeable and can help you find the perfect fit for your hand.
Services: - Custom engraving available - Expert fitting consultation - English-speaking staff - Beautiful gift wrapping
Why Visit: If you want the absolute best selection and quality in Tokyo, this is the place. Perfect for finding special occasion chopsticks or meaningful gifts.



2. Mikura (箸 みくら) ⭐ Kappabashi Favorite
Location: Kappabashi Kitchen Town (Asakusa area)
Address: 3-25-11 Nishi-Asakusa, Taito-ku (2nd floor of Musashiya Seisakusho)
Hours: 10:00 AM - 5:00 PM (closed Sundays and holidays)
Selection: 500+ types
Located in Tokyo’s famous Kitchen Town, Mikura specializes exclusively in chopsticks. They offer natural wood options including ebony, rosewood, iron tree, and bamboo. The shop has a comfortable atmosphere where you can take your time comparing different styles.
Services: - Name engraving available (done on-site) - Staff help with hand-fitting - Chopstick rests and accessories
How to Get There: - 5 min walk from Tsukuba Express “Asakusa” station - 7 min walk from Tokyo Metro Hibiya Line “Iriya” station - 13 min walk from Tokyo Metro Ginza Line “Tawaramachi” station
Why Visit: Great selection at reasonable prices, perfect to combine with exploring Kappabashi Street.



3. Hyozaemon (兵左衛門) ⭐ Traditional Craftsmanship
Location: Hiroo (1 minute from Hiroo Station)
Address: 5-3-9 Cas Bldg. 1F, Hiroo, Shibuya
Selection: Extensive collection from Fukui Prefecture (Japan’s chopstick capital)
Originally founded in Fukui Prefecture (famous for chopstick production), Hyozaemon offers high-quality chopsticks perfect for both everyday use and special occasions. The shop has a refined atmosphere and caters to international visitors.
Services: - Custom engraving: ¥500-800 - ¥500 for single word/name on both chopsticks - ¥800 for name in both English and Japanese - Service takes 10-15 minutes - Gold or black lettering available - Beautiful gift boxes with Mt. Fuji, maneki neko, and other Japanese designs
Why Visit: Excellent middle ground between quality and price, with quick personalization service.



4. Hashito (箸藤)
Location: Kappabashi Kitchen Town
Established: 1910
Selection: Traditional to modern styles
One of Tokyo’s oldest chopstick shops, Hashito offers a wide variety including chopsticks for beginners, children, and bulk disposable options. Great place to see traditional Japanese chopstick craftsmanship.
Why Visit: Historic shop with deep expertise in chopstick making.

title: Chopstick Shopping Guide: Tokyo
section: Kappabashi Kitchen Town (かっぱ橋道具街)
text:
Kappabashi Kitchen Town (かっぱ橋道具街)
What It Is: An 800-meter shopping street with 170+ stores dedicated to kitchen supplies, tableware, and restaurant equipment. Known as “Kitchen Town” or “Tokyo’s Kitchen Supply Paradise.”
Location: Between Asakusa and Ueno in Taito Ward
Chopstick Shopping: Multiple specialty chopstick stores plus many general kitchen shops that carry chopsticks. You’ll find everything from ¥100 budget chopsticks to premium ¥10,000+ artisan pairs.
Other Highlights: - Japanese chef knives - Ceramic tableware - Fake food samples (iconic Tokyo souvenirs) - Chopstick rests in every imaginable design - Professional restaurant supplies
Getting There: - Tawaramachi Station (Tokyo Metro Ginza Line) - 5 min walk (closest) - Asakusa Station (multiple lines) - 10 min walk - Can combine with visiting Sensoji Temple in Asakusa
Shopping Strategy: - Allow 2-3 hours to explore properly - Many shops close on Sundays - Bring cash (some smaller shops don’t take cards) - Store luggage at Kuroneko Yamato counter (¥800/piece) before shopping

title: Chopstick Shopping Guide: Tokyo
section: What to Look For When Buying Chopsticks
text:
What to Look For When Buying Chopsticks

Material Matters
Natural Wood: - Ebony: Elegant, durable, smooth finish - Rosewood: Beautiful grain, comfortable weight - Bamboo: Lightweight, eco-friendly, traditional - Lacquerware: Beautiful finish, easy to clean - Iron tree: Heavy, luxurious feel
Dishwasher-Safe Options: Some modern chopsticks are dishwasher-safe - ask if this matters to you.


Finding the Right Fit
Japanese chopsticks come in different lengths and thicknesses: - Standard adult length: 23-24cm - Women’s/smaller hands: 21-22cm - Men’s/larger hands: 24-25cm - Children’s chopsticks: Various sizes available
Fitting Test: At specialty shops, staff can help you test different lengths and weights to find what feels comfortable. The chopsticks should extend about 1.5 times your hand width.


Price Ranges

Budget: ¥100-500 - Basic everyday chopsticks
Mid-range: ¥1,000-3,000 - Good quality, nice designs
Premium: ¥5,000-10,000 - Artisan crafted, beautiful materials
Luxury: ¥10,000+ - Master craftsman pieces, rare woods

title: Chopstick Shopping Guide: Tokyo
text:
Personalization & Engraving
Many specialty shops offer name engraving services:
Typical Service: - Time: 10-15 minutes (while you wait) - Cost: ¥300-800 per pair - Options: - English or Japanese characters - Gold or black lettering - Your name, dates, or short messages
Great For: - Gifts for family and friends - Wedding favors or anniversary gifts - Commemorating your trip to Japan - Making them uniquely identifiable

title: Chopstick Shopping Guide: Tokyo
text:
Department Store Options
If specialty shops feel overwhelming or time is limited:

Tokyu Hands

Multiple Tokyo locations (Shibuya, Shinjuku, Ginza)
Good selection of chopsticks and kitchen items
Name printing service available
English-friendly shopping experience



LOFT

Locations throughout Tokyo
Modern designs and trendy styles
Popular for gifts
Kawaii (cute) designs available

title: Chopstick Shopping Guide: Tokyo
text:
Shopping Tips
✅ Visit specialty shops first - Better selection and expert guidance
✅ Try before you buy - Good shops encourage testing different styles
✅ Consider engraving - Makes them special and identifiable
✅ Buy chopstick rests too - Beautiful designs, inexpensive souvenirs
✅ Check materials - Ask about dishwasher safety if important
✅ Don’t just buy the cheapest - Mid-range chopsticks (¥1,000-3,000) offer great quality
✅ Buy multiple pairs - Makes great gifts and they’re easy to pack

title: Chopstick Shopping Guide: Tokyo
text:
Combining With Other Activities

Kappabashi + Asakusa Route (Half Day)

Morning: Start at Sensoji Temple in Asakusa
Mid-morning: Walk to Kappabashi (10 min)
Late morning-afternoon: Shop Kappabashi Kitchen Town
Afternoon: Visit Mikura for chopsticks
Evening: Return to Asakusa for dinner at Nakamise Street



Ginza Shopping Route

Visit Ginza Natsuno for premium chopsticks
Explore Ginza’s department stores and shops
Enjoy tea or coffee in Ginza cafes

title: Chopstick Shopping Guide: Tokyo
text:
Quick Recommendations
Best Overall: Ginza Natsuno - The ultimate selection and quality
Best Value: Mikura in Kappabashi - Great selection, reasonable prices
Best for Gifts: Hyozaemon - Beautiful engraving and gift boxes
Best for Variety: Kappabashi Kitchen Town - Multiple stores to compare
Easiest Access: Tokyu Hands or LOFT - Convenient locations

title: Chopstick Shopping Guide: Tokyo
text:
Japanese Chopstick Etiquette (Bonus)
When using your new chopsticks back home:

❌ Don’t stick chopsticks upright in rice (funeral ritual)
❌ Don’t pass food chopstick-to-chopstick (funeral ritual)
❌ Don’t point with chopsticks
❌ Don’t spear food with chopsticks
✅ Rest chopsticks on a chopstick rest when not using
✅ Hold them about 1/3 from the top
✅ Use serving chopsticks for shared dishes

title: Chopstick Shopping Guide: Tokyo
text:
Final Thoughts
Japanese chopsticks make wonderful souvenirs and practical gifts. Unlike many tourist items, they’re useful, beautiful, and authentically Japanese. Whether you spend ¥500 or ¥5,000, you’ll find quality craftsmanship that will last for years.
Take your time, test different styles, and don’t be afraid to ask questions. Most shop owners are passionate about their craft and happy to help you find the perfect pair!
Itadakimasu! (Let’s eat!)

title: Getting Ready
text:
We’re heading to Korea and Japan in 2025, and honestly, the preparation for this trip has become almost as exciting as the trip itself. There’s something special about the anticipation; we’ve never traveled east, nor have we been away from home for 3+ weeks.
So, I decided to build a website to document it all. There’s so much information, too much to keep track of any other way. And way too much to find easily. This way it’s wherever the internet is.

(Plus I need to keep my R / Quarto skills fresh!)

title: Getting Ready
text:
We’re heading to Korea and Japan in 2025, and honestly, the preparation for this trip has become almost as exciting as the trip itself. There’s something special about the anticipation; we’ve never traveled east, nor have we been away from home for 3+ weeks.
So, I decided to build a website to document it all. There’s so much information, too much to keep track of any other way. And way too much to find easily. This way it’s wherever the internet is.

(Plus I need to keep my R / Quarto skills fresh!)

title: Getting Ready
text:
Building the Trip Website

The Idea
So I fired up RStudio and started a Quarto website. It’s become my favorite way to keep a great deal of information together and organized.
The site lives at my domain, updates automatically when I push to GitHub, and has become this living, breathing document of our planning process.
What started as organizational necessity became something more: a creative project, a research repository, and honestly, a way to channel pre-trip excitement into something productive.
The site includes: - Day-by-day itineraries (still being refined) - Research briefs on places we want to visit - Historical context (because how can you visit Korea without understanding the Korean War?) - Shopping guides (stationery! denim!) - Food recommendations - Practical info about power adapters, transportation, money
It’s probably overkill. It’s definitely overkill. But it’s also becoming a valuable resource, and the process of building it has made me think more deeply about what we want from this trip.

title: Getting Ready
text:
Going Down Research Rabbit Holes

The Korean War
I am really looking forward to touring the DMZ. My father was there while in Army shortly after the war ended. Incheon is important, too, as it was the turning point of the war. Did you know General MacArthur’s amphibious assault was considered nearly impossible due to 30-foot tides? That it was precisely because it seemed impossible that it worked?
This isn’t just tourist prep. It’s trying to understand a place before we arrive.


Heyri Art Village
I’d never heard of Heyri until our friend mentioned it in passing. Now I’ve read everything I can find about this artists’ community near the DMZ—370 artists living and working in architecturally unique buildings, creating beauty and culture just miles from the most militarized border in the world. Seems unlikely. That’s why we have to see it!

title: Getting Ready
text:
Conversations with Friends in Korea

The Local Perspective
We’re incredibly fortunate to have good friends living in Seoul. They’ve planned the entire trip! We’ll see places most tourists don’t get to. We’ll also experience Seoul and Incheon with two people who know it intimately.

title: Getting Ready
text:
The Wisconsin Quilt Museum Tour
The Japan portion of our trip is with the Wisconsin Quilt Museum’s art tour, led by Heidi Parkes. This adds a fascinating structure to the experience—we’ll be diving deep into Japanese textile traditions, contemporary art, and cultural sites through a curated lens.

title: Getting Ready
section: What I’m Learning About Travel Planning
text:
What I’m Learning About Travel Planning

1. The Research IS Part of the Experience
I used to think planning was just the necessary logistics before the “real” trip. But diving deep into the history, culture, and details has become its own journey. I’m arriving in Korea and Japan with context, questions, and genuine curiosity—not just a list of sights to check off.


2. Documentation Helps Process Information
Building the website forces me to synthesize what I’m learning. When I write a brief about the Korean War or create a guide to Tokyo stationery shops, I have to really understand it, organize it, make it coherent. It’s active learning, not passive research.

title: Getting Ready
text:
The Website as Travel Companion
One unexpected benefit: the website will travel with us. On our phones in Tokyo, trying to remember which subway line gets us to Nakameguro. In Seoul, double-checking the hours of a museum. At dinner, looking up Korean food terminology so we can order more confidently.
It’s become a living document—part guidebook, part journal, part research archive. And after the trip, it’ll become a memory keeper, a place to add photos and stories and reflections.

title: Getting Ready
text:
What’s Next
There’s still plenty to figure out. Like packing for three weeks.
But the foundation is there. The research is mostly done (though I keep finding new things to add). The excitement is building. And the website is growing day by day.

title: Getting Ready
text:
Why I’m Writing This
Partly to document the process. Partly because I think other people might enjoy reading about the journey-before-the-journey. Partly because writing helps me think.
But mostly because this whole experience—the research, the website building, the conversations with friends, the planning—has reminded me that travel isn’t just about the destination. It’s about curiosity, connection, and the willingness to dive deep into something new.
We’re not just going to Korea and Japan. We’re learning about Korea and Japan. We’re building relationships, asking questions, preparing to show up as engaged visitors rather than passive tourists.
And somehow, building a website about all of it has made the whole process richer, more intentional, more real.

title: Getting Ready
section: See You on the Other Side
text:
See You on the Other Side
By the time you read this (if anyone does), we might already be there. Or maybe we’re still in the thick of planning. Either way, this is the story of how a trip to Korea and Japan became a web development project, a research deep-dive, and a reminder that sometimes the preparation is part of the adventure.

Follow along as we continue to plan, prepare, and eventually experience Korea and Japan. The journey is just beginning—and it started long before we board the plane.

text:
Day 2 of 8
Location: Incheon
Key Activities: Arrival, Incheon night view

title: Heyri Art Village
text: Heyri Art Village is a unique artists’ community located in Paju, just south of the DMZ. Created in 1998, it’s an open-air museum and living arts district where over 370 artists, writers, and creative professionals live and work. The village showcases contemporary Korean art, architecture, and culture in a beautiful, walkable setting.

title: Heyri Art Village
text: Heyri Art Village is a unique artists’ community located in Paju, just south of the DMZ. Created in 1998, it’s an open-air museum and living arts district where over 370 artists, writers, and creative professionals live and work. The village showcases contemporary Korean art, architecture, and culture in a beautiful, walkable setting.

title: Heyri Art Village
text:
What Makes Heyri Special
Architecture as Art - Each building is uniquely designed by different architects - No two structures look alike - Modern, experimental architecture throughout - The village itself is an architectural exhibition.
Living Arts Community - Not just tourist attractions—real artists live and work here - Studios, galleries, museums, performance spaces - Books, films, music, visual arts all represented - Creative energy and authentic artistic vibe.
Proximity to DMZ - Located near the North Korean border - Juxtaposition of art/creativity with military tension - Can combine with DMZ tours - Symbol of hope and culture near division.

title: Heyri Art Village
text:
What to See and Do

Museums and Galleries
Notable Spaces: - Mimesis Art Museum - Contemporary art in stunning minimalist building - Play Museum - Interactive design and toy museum - Jihye’s Story Museum - Children’s book illustrations - Owl Art Museum - Whimsical owl-themed art space - Boan 1942 - Photography and visual arts - Seokparang - Traditional Korean culture meets modern - Dozens of smaller private galleries
Rotating Exhibitions: - Most galleries change exhibits regularly - Check current shows before visiting - Entry fees vary (usually 5,000-10,000 won)


Bookstores and Cultural Spaces

Independent bookstores specializing in art, design, architecture books
Publishing houses with reading rooms
Writer’s studios (some open to public)
Poetry cafes and literary spaces



Cafes and Restaurants
Unique Dining: - Architecturally interesting cafes with art gallery vibes - Specialty coffee roasters - Organic and farm-to-table restaurants - Traditional Korean tea houses - Craft beer pubs
Atmosphere: - Beautiful interiors and exteriors - Many with outdoor seating/terraces - Perfect for lingering and people-watching - Instagram-worthy everywhere


Architecture Walk
Self-Guided Tour: - Simply wander and appreciate the buildings - Each structure tells a story - Mix of concrete, glass, wood, unusual materials - Geometric shapes, curved walls, experimental designs - Great for photography enthusiasts

title: Heyri Art Village
text:
Practical Information

Location
Address: Heyri Village, Paju-si, Gyeonggi-do
Distance from Seoul: About 50km northwest (1-1.5 hours)


Getting There
By Bus: 1. From Seoul: Take bus 200, 2200 from various stations 2. From Hapjeong Station (Line 2/6): Bus 2200 to Heyri 3. From Hapjeong or Hongdae: Bus 200 to Paju, transfer to local bus
By Car/Taxi: - About 1 hour from central Seoul - Parking available throughout village - Taxi from Seoul: 40,000-60,000 won
Tour Options: - Many DMZ tours include Heyri stop - Can hire private driver/guide - Check tour companies for combined packages


Best Time to Visit
Seasons: - Spring (April-May): Cherry blossoms, perfect weather - Fall (October-November): Autumn colors, crisp air - Summer: Hot but green and lush, some outdoor events - Winter: Quiet, stark beauty, some venues may have reduced hours
Day of Week: - Weekdays: Less crowded, more peaceful - Weekends: Busier, more energy, more venues open - Many venues closed Mondays (check specific galleries)
Time Needed: - Half day: Quick walk-through, 1-2 galleries, cafe - Full day: Leisurely exploration, multiple museums, lunch and coffee - Recommended: 4-6 hours


Hours

Village is always open (outdoor space)
Individual galleries/cafes: Usually 10am-6pm or 7pm
Many closed Mondays
Check specific venue hours in advance



Admission

Village entry: Free
Individual museums/galleries: Usually 5,000-10,000 won each
Cafes: Coffee 5,000-8,000 won
Meals: 10,000-20,000 won average

title: Heyri Art Village
text:
Recommended Itinerary

Morning (10am-12pm)

Arrive when venues open
Visit 2-3 museums/galleries of interest
Architecture photography walk
Less crowded in morning



Lunch (12pm-2pm)

Choose from unique restaurants
Indoor or outdoor seating
Try local specialties or organic options



Afternoon (2pm-5pm)

Browse bookstores
Visit more galleries
Explore residential areas (respectfully)
Coffee break at architectural cafe



Optional Evening

Some spaces open later on weekends
Sunset photography
Dinner at special restaurant

title: Heyri Art Village
text:
Tips for Visiting

Planning

Check opening days/hours for specific venues
Download Heyri map (available online)
Wear comfortable walking shoes
Bring camera
Cash helpful (not all places take cards)



Combining with Other Sites
DMZ Tours: - Many tours stop at Heyri - Or visit independently before/after DMZ - Good way to decompress after heavy DMZ experience
Paju Book City: - Another arts/literature complex nearby - Can visit both in one day - Similar creative atmosphere
Nearby: - Odusan Unification Observatory - Imjingak Peace Park - Paju Premium Outlets (if shopping desired)


Photography

Incredible photo opportunities everywhere
Architecture, art, landscapes
Golden hour especially beautiful
Respectful of artists’ workspaces



Etiquette

Many buildings are private residences/studios
Don’t enter spaces marked private
Ask permission before photographing artists at work
Keep noise levels reasonable
Support artists by purchasing if possible

title: Heyri Art Village
text:
Who Will Enjoy Heyri
Perfect For: - Art and architecture enthusiasts - Photographers - Design lovers - Book readers - Anyone wanting alternative to typical tourist sites - Creative types seeking inspiration - Couples (very romantic atmosphere)
Less Suitable For: - Those with very limited mobility (lots of walking, some uneven paths) - People seeking high-energy entertainment - Those uncomfortable with contemporary/experimental art

title: Heyri Art Village
text:
Why Visit

Unique Aspects

Nowhere else like it in Korea
See how Korean artists live and work
Experience cutting-edge Korean architecture
Support independent artists directly
Peaceful escape from Seoul’s bustle



Cultural Insight

Understanding Korean contemporary art scene
Appreciation for design and aesthetics in Korean culture
Post-war artistic expression and freedom
Contrast to both traditional sites and modern Seoul



The DMZ Connection

Art and creativity thriving near conflict zone
Symbol of South Korean freedom and expression
Thought-provoking juxtaposition
Hope and beauty near division

title: Heyri Art Village
section: What to Know Before You Go
text:
What to Know Before You Go

Expectations

This is not Disneyland—it’s a real working artists’ village
Some venues may be closed unexpectedly
Limited English signage in some spaces
Quiet, contemplative atmosphere
Self-directed exploration



Weather Considerations

Much of village is outdoors
Check forecast and dress appropriately
Summer can be very hot
Winter quite cold
Rain makes it less enjoyable



Food and Facilities

Restaurants and cafes throughout
Restrooms at major venues
Limited large grocery stores
Bring water bottle

title: Heyri Art Village
text:
Resources
Planning: - Heyri Art Valley official: www.heyri.net (Korean mostly) - VisitKorea.or.kr has Heyri information in English - Search current exhibitions before visiting - Google Maps works well for navigation within village
Social Media: - Instagram hashtag: #헤이리예술마을 #HeyriArtValley - See current looks and exhibitions - Find specific cafes and galleries

Bottom Line: Heyri Art Village offers a completely different Korean experience—contemporary art, stunning architecture, and creative energy in a peaceful setting near the DMZ. Perfect for a half or full day trip from Seoul, especially for anyone interested in art, design, or simply seeing a unique side of Korean culture.
Bring your camera, wear comfortable shoes, and prepare to be inspired.

title: Korea & Japan 2025
text:
Why Korea & Japan?
These two neighboring countries offer a perfect blend of ancient history and cutting-edge modernity. From Seoul’s vibrant neighborhoods and palaces to Kyoto’s serene temples, from Korean BBQ to fresh sushi, from traditional tea ceremonies to neon-lit streets—we’re ready to experience it all.
The trip combines two distinct experiences: we’ll start in Korea, where we have dear friends who will show us their country through local eyes, followed by a guided cultural and historical tour. Then we’ll continue to Japan as part of the Wisconsin Quilt Museum’s art and culture tour led by Heidi Parkes, exploring Japanese textile traditions, contemporary art, and cultural sites.

title: Korea & Japan 2025
text:
What You’ll Find Here

Top Navigation
Things to See & Do - A curated list of attractions, restaurants, experiences, and hidden gems we’re considering for each destination. This is our master planning list as we finalize our itinerary.
Blog - Real-time updates, photos, and stories as we travel. We’ll share what we’re experiencing, what worked (and what didn’t), and the unexpected discoveries along the way.
Korea Trip Overview and Japan Schedule - High-level summaries of each portion of the trip.


Left Side Navigation
The left side navigation bar includes schedules for the two trips (though the schedules in the top bar are more up-to-date so these may disappear soon) and a section for resources and information.
Day-by-Day Schedules - Use the left sidebar navigation to dive into detailed daily itineraries, including cities, accommodations, planned activities, and our notes as we travel. This is where you’ll find the nitty-gritty of what we’re doing each day.

title: Korea & Japan 2025
text:
Our Approach
We’re fortunate to experience these countries through multiple lenses: the local knowledge of friends in Korea, structured cultural tours, and the Wisconsin Quilt Museum’s carefully curated art itinerary in Japan. We’re blending guided experiences with free time to wander, mixing famous landmarks with local hangouts, and leaving room for spontaneity. The goal is to immerse ourselves in the culture, try as much local food as possible, and return with stories worth telling.
This site is equal parts planning tool and travel journal—a living document that will grow and evolve as we prepare for and embark on this adventure.

Follow along as we explore Korea and Japan. We’ll see you on the other side of the Pacific!

title: A Guide to Korean Food Categories
text: Korean cuisine is renowned for its bold flavors, emphasis on fermentation, and the principle of balance - between hot and cold, spicy and mild, meat and vegetables. This guide covers the major categories of Korean food you’ll encounter during your visit.
crumbs: A Guide to Korean Food Categories

title: A Guide to Korean Food Categories
text: Korean cuisine is renowned for its bold flavors, emphasis on fermentation, and the principle of balance - between hot and cold, spicy and mild, meat and vegetables. This guide covers the major categories of Korean food you’ll encounter during your visit.
crumbs: A Guide to Korean Food Categories

title: A Guide to Korean Food Categories
text:
Kimchi & Banchan (Side Dishes)

Kimchi (김치)
Korea’s most iconic dish - fermented vegetables (usually napa cabbage) seasoned with gochugaru (Korean red pepper flakes), garlic, ginger, and jeotgal (salted seafood). There are hundreds of varieties of kimchi made with different vegetables and seasonings.
Common types: - Baechu kimchi - traditional napa cabbage kimchi - Kkakdugi - cubed radish kimchi - Oi sobagi - cucumber kimchi - Nabak kimchi - water kimchi with radish and napa cabbage


Banchan (반찬)
Small side dishes served complimentary with every Korean meal. Can range from 3-4 dishes at casual restaurants to 10+ at traditional restaurants. Common banchan include seasoned spinach, bean sprouts, fishcake, japchae, and various types of kimchi.
crumbs: A Guide to Korean Food Categories

title: A Guide to Korean Food Categories
text:
Rice Dishes (Bap)

Bibimbap (비빔밥)
A bowl of warm white rice topped with vegetables, beef, a fried egg, and gochujang (red chili paste). Everything is mixed together before eating. Dolsot bibimbap is served in a hot stone bowl, creating a crispy rice crust at the bottom.


Kimbap (김밥)
Korean rice rolls wrapped in seaweed with various fillings like vegetables, egg, pickled radish, and sometimes meat or fish. Often called “Korean sushi” but has distinct flavors and is not vinegared.


Bokkeumbap (볶음밥)
Korean fried rice, often made with kimchi and other ingredients. Frequently served after Korean BBQ using the leftover meat and vegetables on the grill.
crumbs: A Guide to Korean Food Categories

title: A Guide to Korean Food Categories
text:
Noodle Dishes (Myeon)

Japchae (잡채)
Stir-fried sweet potato glass noodles with vegetables and sometimes beef, seasoned with soy sauce and sesame oil. A popular dish for celebrations and holidays.


Naengmyeon (냉면)
Cold buckwheat noodles served in an icy broth or with spicy sauce. Particularly popular in summer and after Korean BBQ as a palate cleanser.


Jajangmyeon (자장면)
Korean-Chinese noodles with a thick black bean sauce. One of Korea’s most beloved comfort foods, often delivered as takeout.


Ramyeon (라면)
Korean instant noodles, but also served fresh at restaurants. Korean ramyeon tends to be spicier than Japanese ramen.
crumbs: A Guide to Korean Food Categories

title: A Guide to Korean Food Categories
text:
Stews & Soups

Jjigae (찌개) - Stews
Thick, hearty stews typically served bubbling in a clay pot or stone bowl.
Popular varieties: - Kimchi jjigae - kimchi stew with pork and tofu - Doenjang jjigae - fermented soybean paste stew with vegetables and tofu - Sundubu jjigae - soft tofu stew with vegetables, often spicy - Budae jjigae - “army base stew” with spam, hot dogs, ramen, and kimchi (originated after Korean War)


Guk & Tang (국 & 탕) - Soups
Lighter broths served with meals.
Common types: - Miyeokguk - seaweed soup (traditionally eaten on birthdays) - Samgyetang - ginseng chicken soup with whole young chicken stuffed with rice - Galbitang - beef short rib soup - Yukgaejang - spicy beef soup with vegetables - Seolleongtang - milky ox bone soup
crumbs: A Guide to Korean Food Categories

title: A Guide to Korean Food Categories
text:
Korean BBQ & Grilled Meats (Gui)

Bulgogi (불고기)
Thinly sliced marinated beef (or pork) grilled at the table. The marinade typically includes soy sauce, sugar, garlic, and pear juice, giving it a sweet and savory flavor.


Galbi (갈비)
Marinated beef or pork short ribs, grilled and cut into pieces. LA galbi refers to ribs cut across the bone in the American style.


Samgyeopsal (삼겹살)
Thick slices of pork belly grilled at the table, typically unseasoned. Wrapped in lettuce leaves with garlic, ssamjang (thick spicy paste), and other accompaniments.


Dakgalbi (닭갈비)
Spicy stir-fried chicken with vegetables, rice cakes, and sweet potatoes, cooked on a large griddle at the table.


Korean BBQ Etiquette

Meat is typically grilled at your table by you or the server
Wrap grilled meat in lettuce or perilla leaves with rice, garlic, and sauces (called ssam)
Use scissors to cut the meat
Don’t flip meat too often - let it cook properly on each side
crumbs: A Guide to Korean Food Categories

title: A Guide to Korean Food Categories
text:
Fried & Savory Foods

Jeon (전) / Buchimgae (부침개)
Korean savory pancakes made with various ingredients.
Popular varieties: - Pajeon - green onion pancake - Haemul pajeon - seafood and green onion pancake - Kimchijeon - kimchi pancake - Bindaetteok - mung bean pancake


Korean Fried Chicken (치킨)
Korea’s famous twice-fried chicken, known for its ultra-crispy coating. Often served with beer (called chimaek = chicken + maekju/beer).
Common styles: - Yangnyeom chicken - coated in sweet and spicy sauce - Ganjang chicken - soy garlic chicken - Huraideu chicken - plain fried chicken - Banban - half and half (usually half plain, half seasoned)


Tteokbokki (떡볶이)
Chewy rice cakes in spicy-sweet red sauce. A quintessential Korean street food. Often served with odeng (fish cakes) and twigim (fried items like vegetables, dumplings, or squid).


Mandu (만두)
Korean dumplings filled with meat and vegetables, served steamed (jjin mandu), fried (gun mandu), or in soup (mandu guk).
crumbs: A Guide to Korean Food Categories

title: A Guide to Korean Food Categories
text:
Street Food & Snacks

Hotteok (호떡)
Sweet griddled pancakes filled with brown sugar, cinnamon, and nuts. A popular winter street snack.


Bungeoppang (붕어빵)
Fish-shaped pastry filled with sweet red bean paste.


Eomuk / Odeng (어묵/오뎅)
Fish cakes on skewers, often served in broth at street stalls.


Gimbap (김밥)
As mentioned above, but commonly sold at street vendors and convenience stores as a snack or quick meal.


Sundae (순대)
Korean blood sausage made with glass noodles and barley. Despite the name, not related to the ice cream dessert!
crumbs: A Guide to Korean Food Categories

title: A Guide to Korean Food Categories
text:
Desserts & Sweet Treats

Bingsu (빙수)
Shaved ice dessert topped with sweet toppings. - Patbingsu - traditional version with sweet red beans, rice cakes, and condensed milk - Injeolmi bingsu - with roasted soybean powder - Modern versions with fruit, ice cream, and various toppings


Tteok (떡)
Korean rice cakes made from glutinous rice. - Songpyeon - crescent-shaped, filled with sweet ingredients - Injeolmi - coated in roasted soybean powder - Chapssaltteok - filled with sweet red bean paste


Yakgwa (약과)
Deep-fried honey cookies with a flower pattern, crispy outside and chewy inside.


Hodugwaja (호두과자)
Walnut-shaped pastries filled with sweet red bean paste and walnut pieces.
crumbs: A Guide to Korean Food Categories

title: A Guide to Korean Food Categories
text:
Beverages

Traditional Drinks

Soju (소주)
Korea’s most popular alcoholic beverage - a clear distilled spirit (16-20% ABV). Traditionally made from rice but now often from sweet potatoes. Served in shot glasses and consumed in one shot.
Drinking etiquette: - Pour drinks for others, not yourself - Use two hands when pouring or receiving from elders - Turn your head away when drinking in front of elders


Makgeolli (막걸리)
Milky, slightly sweet rice wine with a low alcohol content (6-8%). Traditional farmers’ drink.


Korean Tea (차)

Nokcha - green tea
Insam-cha - ginseng tea
Yujacha - yuzu citrus tea (often served as a hot sweet drink)
Boricha - roasted barley tea (commonly served as water in restaurants)




Modern Beverages

Banana Milk (바나나맛 우유)
Iconic Korean flavored milk in a distinctive yellow bottle. Available at all convenience stores.


Korean Coffee Culture
Korea has an extremely vibrant coffee culture with countless cafes. Popular chains include: - Ediya Coffee - affordable chain - Mega Coffee - known for large sizes - Paik’s Coffee - celebrity-owned chain
crumbs: A Guide to Korean Food Categories

title: A Guide to Korean Food Categories
text:
Regional Specialties

Seoul

Seolleongtang - ox bone soup
Kongnamul gukbap - bean sprout rice soup



Jeonju (전주)

Jeonju bibimbap - considered the best bibimbap in Korea
Kongnamul gukbap - bean sprout soup



Busan (부산)

Milmyeon - wheat noodles in cold broth
Ssiat hotteok - filled with seeds and nuts



Jeju Island (제주도)

Heuk dwaeji - black pork BBQ
Jeonbok juk - abalone porridge
crumbs: A Guide to Korean Food Categories

title: A Guide to Korean Food Categories
text:
Dietary Considerations

Vegetarian Options
Korean cuisine traditionally includes many vegetables, but many dishes contain meat or seafood-based broths and seasonings. Temple food (like you’ll experience at Geumsunsa) is completely vegetarian/vegan.
Look for: - Bibimbap (ask for no meat) - Japchae (vegetable version) - Kimchi jjigae (ask for no pork) - Dubu jorim - braised tofu
Note: Many kimchi varieties contain fish sauce or shrimp paste. Ask for “mukeunji” for older, milder kimchi that’s often vegetarian.


Spice Levels
Korean food can be quite spicy. If sensitive to spice, learn these phrases: - “Maepji ankae juseyo” (맵지 않게 주세요) - “Not spicy please” - “Maeweoyo” (매워요) - “It’s spicy”
crumbs: A Guide to Korean Food Categories

title: A Guide to Korean Food Categories
text:
Food Etiquette & Customs

Table Manners

Wait for the eldest person to start eating first
Don’t pick up your rice or soup bowl (unlike Japanese custom)
Use a spoon for rice and soup, chopsticks for side dishes
Don’t stick chopsticks upright in rice (funeral ritual)
It’s polite to finish everything on your plate



Dining Out

Banchan is unlimited - you can ask for more of any side dish
Call servers with “Yogi-yo!” (여기요!) or press the call button on the table
Tipping is not customary in Korea
Many restaurants specialize in just one dish
Split the bill is less common; one person typically pays



Korean BBQ Specific

Servers may cut the meat and grill for you
Don’t flip meat constantly - let it cook
Use the scissors to cut meat into bite-sized pieces
Wrap meat in lettuce with rice, garlic, and ssamjang
crumbs: A Guide to Korean Food Categories

title: A Guide to Korean Food Categories
text:
Restaurant Recommendations by Area

Insadong Area

Traditional tea houses
Samgyetang (ginseng chicken soup) restaurants
Korean dessert cafes



Itaewon

International cuisine
Fusion restaurants
Late-night dining options

Street food stalls

Hotteok stands



Myeongdong

Street food heaven
Korean fried chicken

crumbs: A Guide to Korean Food Categories

title: A Guide to Korean Food Categories
text:
Tips for Food Adventurers

Try temple food at Geumsunsa - it’s a unique vegetarian Buddhist cuisine experience
Visit a traditional market like Namdaemun for authentic street food
Do Korean BBQ properly - at least once, go to a mid-range restaurant where they grill for you
Have a meal at a convenience store - sounds odd, but Korean convenience stores have surprisingly good instant noodles, kimbap, and triangular rice balls
Try regional specialties - if visiting Jeonju, don’t miss the bibimbap
Go to a pojangmacha - street tent bars for soju and snacks
Experience a meal service (백반) - traditional meal with rice, soup, stew, and many banchan
crumbs: A Guide to Korean Food Categories

title: A Guide to Korean Food Categories
text:
Food Safety & Practical Notes

Tap water is safe to drink throughout Korea
Restaurants are generally very clean with high food safety standards
Most restaurants don’t accept reservations - expect to wait at popular spots
Many places close between lunch and dinner (around 3-5 PM)
Convenience stores are 24/7 and have ATMs, snacks, and basic necessities
Food delivery is incredibly advanced - apps like Coupang Eats and Baemin deliver almost anything
crumbs: A Guide to Korean Food Categories

title: A Guide to Korean Food Categories
text:
Must-Try Checklist
Before you leave Korea, make sure you’ve tried:

Korean BBQ (samgyeopsal or galbi)
Bibimbap in a stone bowl
Kimchi jjigae or sundubu jjigae
Korean fried chicken with beer (chimaek)
Fresh kimbap
Street food tteokbokki
Hotteok (sweet pancake)
Soju (responsibly!)
Patbingsu (in season)
Temple food at Geumsunsa
Pajeon on a rainy day
Korean convenience store instant noodles

Enjoy your culinary adventure in Korea! 맛있게 드세요! (Masitge deuseyo - Enjoy your meal!)

title: Korean War Sites
text:
A guide to historical sites related to the Korean War that you can visit during your trip to South Korea.




Location: Yongsan District, Seoul
Subway: Samgakji Station (Lines 4, 6), Exit 12
What’s There: - Massive museum covering Korean military history, with major focus on Korean War - Outdoor displays of tanks, aircraft, and artillery - Memorial Hall with names of fallen soldiers - Detailed exhibits on major battles, including Incheon Landing - Personal stories, artifacts, photographs - Interactive displays and dioramas
Why Visit: - Most comprehensive Korean War museum in the country - Well-presented with English translations - Free admission - Plan 2-3 hours minimum
Highlights: - The 6.25 Room (June 25 is when war started) - Incheon Landing exhibit - “Brothers” statue at entrance (symbol of divided families)




Location: Itaewon area, Seoul
What’s There: - Outdoor memorial and monuments - Peaceful reflection space - View of city from elevated position
Why Visit: - Quick stop, no admission fee - Good for reflection after the main museum - 30 minutes







Location: Yeonsu District, Incheon (near Incheon Harbor)
What’s There: - Museum dedicated specifically to Operation Chromite (Incheon Landing) - Dioramas of the amphibious assault - MacArthur memorabilia and historical documents - Scale models of the landing - Film presentations - Observation deck overlooking the harbor where the landing occurred
Why Visit: - Standing where history was made - See the actual harbor and understand the tidal challenges - Relatively small, focused museum (1-2 hours) - Can combine with Incheon Chinatown visit
Getting There: - Incheon Metro to Dongincheon Station - About 1 hour from Seoul




Location: Freedom Park (Jayu Park), Incheon
What’s There: - Large statue of General MacArthur overlooking Incheon Harbor - The actual vantage point where he observed the landing - Park setting with harbor views
Why Visit: - Photo opportunity - Historical perspective on the landing site - Can walk up from Chinatown - Free, 15-30 minutes







Location: About 1 hour north of Seoul
What’s There (varies by tour): - 3rd Infiltration Tunnel (North Korean tunnel discovered in 1978) - Dora Observatory (look into North Korea with binoculars) - Dorasan Station (northernmost train station, built for hoped-for reunification) - Joint Security Area (JSA/Panmunjom) - where armistice was signed - Freedom Bridge - DMZ Museum.
Why Visit: - See the ongoing legacy of the Korean War - Most fortified border in the world - Surreal experience of being at the edge of North Korea - Understand the continued division.
Important Notes: - MUST book organized tour - cannot visit independently - JSA tours require advance booking (weeks ahead) and passport - Dress code enforced (no ripped jeans, no sandals, no military-style clothing) - Photography restrictions - Tours run daily but book early - Full day trip (6-8 hours).
Recommended Tour Companies: - Koridoor Tours - VIP Travel - USO Tours (if available to civilians).
Cost: - Basic DMZ tour: $40-80 - JSA inclusion: $80-150







Location: Nam District, Busan
Subway: Daeyeon Station (Line 2)
What’s There: - Only UN cemetery in the world - 2,300 graves from 11 countries - Memorial monuments for each participating nation - Beautiful, peaceful grounds - Museum documenting UN forces’ role.
Why Visit: - Sobering reminder of international sacrifice - Beautiful, contemplative space - Well-maintained grounds - Free admission - 1-2 hours.
Unique Aspect: - Many families still visit from around the world - Annual memorial ceremonies.




Location: Jung District, Busan
What’s There: - Exhibits on Busan during the war (temporary capital of South Korea) - Refugee experience during Pusan Perimeter battle - City’s transformation during war - Personal stories of survival.
Why Visit: - Understand Busan’s critical role as last stronghold - Local perspective on the war - Small but well-curated - 1 hour.







Location: Gangwon Province, northeast of Seoul.
What’s There: - Former battlefields - Abandoned buildings in former DMZ - “Iron Triangle” area of heavy fighting - Less touristy than main DMZ.
Why Visit: - More authentic feel - See actual battlefields - For serious history buffs - Requires more planning/transportation.




Location: South of Seoul
What’s There: - Songtan entertainment district (near US air base) - Historical sites related to early war battles - Not a formal museum.
Why Visit: - Only if you have specific interest or contacts - See ongoing US military presence.







Must See (if time for only 1-2): 1. War Memorial of Korea (Seoul) - Most comprehensive 2. DMZ Tour - See ongoing legacy
If You Have More Time: 3. Incheon Landing Memorial - Understand the turning point 4. UN Cemetery (if in Busan) - Moving tribute



Best Days: - Most museums closed Mondays - DMZ tours available most days but book ahead - Check Korean holidays (sites may be closed)
Language: - Major sites have English signage and audio guides - DMZ tours typically have English-speaking guides
Costs: - Most museums: Free or very cheap (5,000-10,000 won = $4-8) - DMZ tours: More expensive but worth it
Transportation: - Seoul metro is excellent and cheap - Incheon accessible via metro - DMZ requires tour bus - Busan sites accessible via metro



Half Day Seoul: - War Memorial of Korea (2-3 hours) - Korean War Memorial Park (30 min)
Full Day DMZ: - DMZ tour from Seoul (6-8 hours including transport)
Incheon Day Trip: - Incheon Landing Memorial (2 hours) - MacArthur Statue/Freedom Park (1 hour) - Incheon Chinatown (lunch and wandering)
If in Busan: - UN Cemetery (1-2 hours) - Modern History Museum (1 hour)





When visiting these sites: - Be respectful—this is living history for Koreans - Many older Koreans lived through the war - Millions of families remain separated - The wound is still fresh, not ancient history - Photography usually allowed but be tasteful - At DMZ, follow all rules strictly (security concerns).



Booking DMZ Tours: - Book 1-2 weeks ahead (JSA tours need more time) - Bring passport for DMZ visits - Check current security situation.
More Information: - War Memorial: www.warmemo.or.kr (English version) - Korea Tourism Organization: english.visitkorea.or.kr.

The Korean War shaped everything about modern Korea. These sites offer windows into that history and its continuing impact.

title: Power & Electrical Guide: Korea & Japan Trip
text:
Plug Types

Japan

Type A & B (same as US)
Your US plugs will fit directly into Japanese outlets
No adapter needed



South Korea

Type C & F (European-style round pins)
Your US plugs will NOT fit
Adapter required
crumbs: Power & Electrical Guide: Korea & Japan Trip

title: Power & Electrical Guide: Korea & Japan Trip
text:
What You Need to Bring

Essential: Universal Travel Adapter
Bring at least one universal travel adapter that includes: - Type C/F plugs for Korea - Multiple USB ports (very helpful)
Recommended brands: - EPICKA Universal Travel Adapter - TESSAN Universal Travel Adapter - Anker USB Wall Charger with international adapters


Optional: Power Strip
Consider bringing a US power strip (3-4 outlets): - Plug the strip into your adapter in Korea - Allows you to charge multiple US devices from one adapter - Works with your existing US charging cables - Only need ONE adapter this way
crumbs: Power & Electrical Guide: Korea & Japan Trip

title: Power & Electrical Guide: Korea & Japan Trip
text:
Your Devices: What Works Where

✅ No Converter Needed (Just Adapter for Korea)
Most modern electronics have dual-voltage power supplies (100-240V):

Laptops - Check the power brick; it should say “100-240V”
Phones - All modern smartphones are dual voltage
Tablets (iPad, etc.) - Dual voltage
Camera battery chargers - Usually dual voltage
Electric toothbrush chargers - Check label
Power banks - No issue (they charge via USB)

How to check: Look for “INPUT: 100-240V” on the device or power adapter


⚠️ May Need Converter
High-wattage heat-producing devices (rarely dual voltage):

Hair dryers
Curling irons
Straighteners

Recommendation: Don’t bring these. Buy cheap ones in Korea/Japan if needed, or use hotel-provided hair dryers.
crumbs: Power & Electrical Guide: Korea & Japan Trip

title: Power & Electrical Guide: Korea & Japan Trip
text:
Practical Tips

In Japan

Your plugs fit directly - no adapter needed
Some older Japanese outlets only accept 2-prong (Type A), not 3-prong (Type B)
Voltage is slightly lower (100V vs US 120V) but this is fine for modern electronics



In Korea

Bring 1-2 adapters, or 1 adapter + US power strip
Hotels often provide adapters at the front desk (ask!)
Convenience stores (GS25, CU, 7-Eleven) sell adapters if needed



General

Download offline maps before you go
Consider bringing a portable charger (10,000+ mAh)
Many cafes and convenience stores have charging spots
Trains and buses often have USB charging ports
crumbs: Power & Electrical Guide: Korea & Japan Trip

title: Power & Electrical Guide: Korea & Japan Trip
text:
Shopping List

Must Have

1-2 universal travel adapters with Type C/F



Nice to Have

US power strip (3-4 outlets)
Portable battery bank (10,000-20,000 mAh)
Extra USB charging cables
USB wall adapter with multiple ports



Don’t Bother

Voltage converter (unless you have specific 120V-only devices)
Hair dryer/straightener (use hotel’s or buy there)
crumbs: Power & Electrical Guide: Korea & Japan Trip

title: Power & Electrical Guide: Korea & Japan Trip
text:
Questions to Ask Yourself
Before packing, check your devices:

Does my laptop charger say “100-240V”? (It almost certainly does)
How many devices will I need to charge simultaneously?
Will I need to work/charge in cafes or only in hotels?
Is my portable charger fully charged?

Have a great trip!

title: Japanese Stationary
text:
Must-Visit Shops

Traveler’s Factory (Nakameguro)
The Holy Grail for Traveler’s Notebook Fans
Location: Nakameguro, Tokyo
Nearest Station: Nakameguro Station (Hibiya Line)
Address: 〒153-0051 Tokyo, Meguro City, Kamimeguro, 3 Chome−13−10
From Hotel East 21: - Toyocho Station (Tozai Line) → Otemachi (transfer) → Hibiya Line to Nakameguro - Time: 35-40 minutes - Alternative: Toyocho → Nihombashi (transfer) → Hibiya Line to Nakameguro - Distance: About 12km
What Makes It Special: - Official flagship store of Traveler’s Company - Original Traveler’s Notebook leather covers in all sizes - Exclusive limited editions and collaborations - Every refill, accessory, and customization option - Vintage brass accessories - Factory Exclusive items you can’t get anywhere else - Watch craftspeople work on notebooks - Small cafe upstairs (coffee and atmosphere)
What to Buy: - Traveler’s Notebook leather covers (passport, regular, A5 sizes) - Refills in every style (blank, lined, grid, kraft, watercolor) - Brass clips, rulers, pens - Stickers, stamps, decorative items - Limited edition seasonal items - Customization services
Tips: - Go early or late to avoid crowds - Allow 1-2 hours to browse properly - Bring extra luggage space - Staff speak English - Very Instagrammable location
Hours: Usually 12pm-8pm (check current hours)
Website: travelers-factory.com



Itoya (Ginza)
12 Floors of Stationery Heaven
Location: Ginza, Tokyo
Nearest Station: Ginza Station (multiple lines)
Address: 2-7-15 Ginza, Chuo-ku, Tokyo
From Hotel East 21: - Toyocho Station (Tozai Line) → Direct to Nihombashi (3 stops) → Walk 10 min OR transfer to Ginza Line (1 stop to Ginza) - Time: 20-25 minutes total - Easiest route: Toyocho → Otemachi (2 stops) → Transfer to any line to Ginza (2 stops) - Distance: About 8km - This is one of your closest major shops!
What Makes It Special: - Iconic Tokyo stationery store since 1904 - 12 floors dedicated to different stationery categories - Incredible mechanical pencil selection - High-end and everyday items - Beautiful building, each floor color-coded - Main store + K.Itoya annex across the street
Floor Guide: - B1F: Greeting cards, gift wrap - 1F: Fountain pens, luxury writing instruments - 2F: Japanese paper products, washi - 3F: Mechanical pencils, ballpoint pens, pencils - 4F: Notebooks, journals, planners - 5F: Business supplies, desk accessories - 6F-11F: Various specialized items - 12F: Red Cafe with Ginza views
What to Buy: - Premium mechanical pencils (Pentel, Pilot, Uni, Zebra, Rotring) - Midori MD Notebooks - Hobonichi planners and notebooks - Washi tape and stickers - High-quality fountain pens - Unique desk accessories
Tips: - Start at top and work down, or focus on floors 3-4 for pencils/notebooks - Check out K.Itoya annex too (more lifestyle/design items) - Tax-free shopping available with passport - Can easily spend 2-3 hours here
Hours: 10am-8pm (Mon-Sat), 10am-7pm (Sun)
Website: www.ito-ya.co.jp



Tokyu Hands (Multiple Locations)
Department Store Meets DIY Haven
Best Location: Shibuya (8 floors)
Other Locations: Shinjuku, Ikebukuro, many more
From Hotel East 21 to Shibuya: - Toyocho Station (Tozai Line) → Otemachi → Transfer to Hanzomon Line → Shibuya - Time: 35-40 minutes - Alternative: Via Nihombashi with transfers - Distance: About 15km
From Hotel East 21 to Shinjuku: - Nishi-ojima Station (Toei Shinjuku Line) → Direct to Shinjuku-sanchome - Time: 25-30 minutes (this is your EASIEST route!) - Distance: About 12km - Shinjuku Tokyu Hands is most convenient for you!
What Makes It Special: - Huge department store with amazing stationery floor - Mid-range prices - Great variety without overwhelming - Easy to navigate - Multiple locations across Tokyo
What to Buy: - Good selection of mechanical pencils (all major brands) - Notebooks and planners - Masking tape and craft supplies - Unique Japanese organizational tools - DIY and craft materials - Practical everyday stationery
Tips: - Shibuya location is largest and most comprehensive - Stationery usually on 5th or 6th floor - Less overwhelming than some specialty shops - Good for “I need to pick up various items” shopping
Hours: Vary by location, typically 10am-9pm



Loft (Multiple Locations)
Trendy Lifestyle & Stationery
Best Locations: Shibuya, Ginza, Ikebukuro
Flagship: Shibuya Loft (7 floors)
From Hotel East 21: - To Ginza Loft: Same as Itoya above (20-25 minutes) - CLOSEST - To Shibuya Loft: 35-40 minutes via Tozai Line → Otemachi → Hanzomon Line - To Ikebukuro: Toyocho → Otemachi → Marunouchi Line to Ikebukuro (30 min) - Recommendation: Visit Ginza Loft - it’s your closest option!
What Makes It Special: - Curated selection of trendy stationery - Japanese and imported brands - Design-forward products - Good prices - Hip, young vibe
What to Buy: - Midori products (MD notebooks, Traveler’s items) - Cute Japanese character stationery - Innovative desk tools - Imported stationery brands - Washi tape paradise - Planners and organizers
Tips: - Stationery usually takes up 1-2 full floors - Great for gifts - Less “serious” than Itoya, more fun/colorful - Tax-free shopping available
Hours: Typically 10am-9pm



Kakimori (Kuramae)
Custom Notebooks & Artisan Ink
Location: Kuramae, Tokyo (trendy artisan neighborhood)
Nearest Station: Kuramae Station (Asakusa or Oedo Line)
From Hotel East 21: - Toyocho Station (Tozai Line) → Nihombashi → Transfer to Asakusa Line → Kuramae - Time: 25-30 minutes - Alternative: Tozai Line → Monzen-nakacho → Oedo Line → Kuramae
- Distance: About 6km - This is relatively close to your hotel!
What Makes It Special: - Custom notebook service - choose paper, binding, cover - Custom ink blending (create your own fountain pen ink color!) - Artisan, handcrafted focus - Beautiful minimal aesthetic - Small but perfectly curated
What to Buy: - Custom-made notebooks (takes 30-60 minutes) - Blended custom ink - Writing instruments - Limited selection but highest quality - Unique gifts
Tips: - Plan for time if doing custom notebook - Great for serious stationery enthusiasts - Combine with exploring Kuramae neighborhood (cafes, vintage shops) - English spoken
Hours: 11am-7pm (closed Mondays)
Website: kakimori.com



Maruzen (Multiple Locations)
Books + Premium Stationery
Best Location: Maruzen Marunouchi (near Tokyo Station)
From Hotel East 21: - Toyocho Station (Tozai Line) → Direct to Otemachi (2 stops, 5 min) → Walk 10 min to Marunouchi - Time: 15-20 minutes total - Distance: About 6km - This is your CLOSEST major shop - super convenient! - Can also get off at Tokyo Station (one more stop) and walk
What Makes It Special: - Traditional bookstore with excellent stationery section - High-quality selection - Less touristy than Itoya - Connected to Japanese literary culture - Elegant atmosphere
What to Buy: - Quality mechanical pencils - Fountain pens - Traditional Japanese paper products - Business journals and planners - Gift-quality items
Tips: - Great if you also want English books about Japan - More refined, less overwhelming - Good for serious writing enthusiasts



Sekaido (Shinjuku)
Art Supplies + Stationery
Location: Shinjuku
Nearest Station: Shinjuku Station
From Hotel East 21: - Nishi-ojima Station (Toei Shinjuku Line) → Direct to Shinjuku-sanchome (no transfers!) - Time: 25-30 minutes - Distance: About 12km - Easy direct route - no transfers needed!
What Makes It Special: - Art supplies meet stationery - 5 floors - Great prices - Less touristy - Locals’ favorite
What to Buy: - Mechanical pencils (including drafting pencils) - Sketch books and art notebooks - Technical drawing supplies - Manga supplies - Affordable quality items
Tips: - Good for artists and designers - More “working supplies” than “precious stationery” - Solid everyday prices

title: Japanese Stationary
text:
Specialty Items Guide

Mechanical Pencils
Where to Find Best Selection: 1. Itoya (Floor 3) - Largest variety, all price points 2. Tokyu Hands - Good mid-range selection 3. Sekaido - Technical/drafting pencils
Japanese Brands to Look For: - Pentel: GraphGear 500/1000, Orenz Nero (unbreakable lead) - Pilot: S20, S10, S5, S3 - Uni (Mitsubishi): Kuru Toga (rotating lead mechanism), Shift - Zebra: DelGuard (lead break protection) - Platinum: MSD series, Pro-Use 171 - Ohto: Conception, Promecha - Tombow: Mono Graph series
Price Ranges: - Budget: ¥300-800 ($2-6) - Mid-range: ¥800-3,000 ($6-20) - Premium: ¥3,000-10,000+ ($20-70+)
Unique Japanese Features: - Rotating lead mechanisms (Kuru Toga) - Lead break prevention systems - Ultra-precise lead grades (2H to 4B) - 0.2mm ultra-fine leads - Ergonomic grip designs



Traveler’s Notebooks
Where to Buy: 1. Traveler’s Factory - THE place, exclusive items 2. Itoya - Good selection, regular line 3. Loft - Decent selection, accessories 4. Tokyu Hands - Basic selection
What to Get: - Leather Covers: Passport, Regular (A5), or A4 sizes - Refills: Choose your paper style (grid, lined, blank, kraft, watercolor) - Brass Accessories: Clips, pen holders, rulers - Charms and Customization: Make it yours
Traveler’s Factory Exclusives: - Limited edition colors - Special collaborations - Factory-stamping service - Unique refills



Other Japanese Stationery to Explore
Notebooks: - Midori MD Notebook - Minimal design, excellent paper - Hobonichi Techo - Cult-favorite planner - Life Noble Note - Premium writing paper - Apica Premium CD - Smooth writing experience
Pens: - Pilot Juice - Smooth gel pens - Uni-ball Signo - Waterproof, fade-proof - Zebra Sarasa - Quick-dry gel ink - Pentel Energel - Fast-drying
Unique Items: - Washi Tape (mt brand is iconic) - Sticky Notes (creative shapes and designs) - Correction Tape (Japanese engineering at its finest) - Page Flags (aesthetic and functional) - Desk Organizers (minimal, beautiful)

title: Japanese Stationary
text:
Shopping Strategy

From Hotel East 21: Proximity Guide
Closest & Easiest (15-25 minutes): 1. Maruzen Marunouchi - 15-20 min via Tozai Line direct 2. Itoya Ginza - 20-25 min via Tozai Line → Otemachi 3. Ginza Loft - 20-25 min (same route as Itoya) 4. Kakimori Kuramae - 25-30 min via Tozai/Asakusa Lines
Medium Distance (25-35 minutes): 5. Shinjuku (Tokyu Hands, Sekaido) - 25-30 min via Toei Shinjuku Line direct (no transfers!)
Farther but Worth It (35-40 minutes): 6. Traveler’s Factory Nakameguro - 35-40 min (requires transfer) 7. Shibuya (Tokyu Hands, Loft) - 35-40 min (requires transfer)


Recommended Day Trips from Hotel East 21
Easy Half Day - Ginza Focus: - Morning: Maruzen Marunouchi (15 min) - Walk to Itoya (10 min walk) - Lunch in Ginza - Ginza Loft if time permits - All via Tozai Line - super easy!
Shinjuku Day: - Direct Toei Shinjuku Line (no transfers!) - Tokyu Hands + Sekaido + shopping/sightseeing - Easiest single-destination trip
Art & Craft Day - Kuramae: - Kakimori (25 min) - Explore artisan neighborhood - Close to hotel, easy return
Full Day - Traveler’s Notebook Pilgrimage: - Morning: Traveler’s Factory Nakameguro - Afternoon: Return via Shibuya (Tokyu Hands/Loft) - Evening: Back to hotel


Time-Based Approach
If You Have 2-3 Hours: - Go to Itoya (most comprehensive) - Or Traveler’s Factory (if Traveler’s notebooks are priority)
If You Have Half a Day: - Morning: Traveler’s Factory in Nakameguro - Afternoon: Itoya in Ginza - Stop at Loft if time permits
If You Have a Full Day: - Morning: Kakimori custom notebook experience - Lunch in Kuramae - Afternoon: Itoya for main shopping - Evening: Shibuya Loft or Tokyu Hands


Budget Planning
Budget Friendly: - Tokyu Hands, Loft, Sekaido - Expect: ¥5,000-10,000 ($35-70) for good haul
Mid-Range: - Mix of above + some premium items from Itoya - Expect: ¥15,000-30,000 ($100-200)
Splurge: - Traveler’s Factory complete setup - Premium mechanical pencils from Itoya - Custom notebooks from Kakimori - Expect: ¥30,000-50,000+ ($200-350+)


Tax-Free Shopping
Requirements: - Passport required - Minimum purchase: ¥5,000-10,000 (varies by store) - Present at time of purchase - Items must leave Japan with you
Available At: - Itoya - Loft - Tokyu Hands - Most major retailers

title: Japanese Stationary
text:
Practical Tips

Language

Major stores have English-speaking staff
Product names often in English/Japanese
Google Translate camera function helps
Point and smile works great!



Payment

Credit cards widely accepted at major stores
Cash still preferred at smaller shops
ATMs at convenience stores



Carrying Your Haul

Bring a foldable bag
Consider shipping large purchases to hotel
Many stores offer gift wrapping (free or small fee)



Best Times to Visit

Weekday mornings - least crowded
Avoid weekends if possible at popular spots
Holiday seasons (Jan, Golden Week) can be very busy



Combining with Sightseeing
Ginza Day: - Itoya → Ginza shopping → Imperial Palace nearby
Shibuya/Harajuku Day: - Loft/Tokyu Hands → Shibuya crossing → Meiji Shrine
Nakameguro Day: - Traveler’s Factory → Meguro River walk → Daikanyama cafes
Kuramae Artisan Day: - Kakimori → Vintage shops → Sumida River → Asakusa

Sample Shopping List:
Mechanical Pencil Enthusiast: - 3-4 mid-range pencils: ¥5,000 ($35) - Premium pencil: ¥5,000 ($35) - Lead refills variety pack: ¥1,500 ($10) - Total: ~¥11,500 ($80)
Traveler’s Notebook Starter: - Regular leather cover: ¥5,000 ($35) - 3-4 refills: ¥2,000 ($14) - Brass accessories: ¥3,000 ($20) - Customization: ¥1,000 ($7) - Total: ~¥11,000 ($76)
Comprehensive Haul: - 2-3 mechanical pencils: ¥8,000 ($55) - Traveler’s notebook setup: ¥10,000 ($70) - Midori MD notebooks: ¥3,000 ($20) - Pens and accessories: ¥5,000 ($35) - Washi tape and extras: ¥4,000 ($28) - Total: ~¥30,000 ($208)

title: Japanese Stationary
section: Making the Most of Your Visit
text:
Making the Most of Your Visit

Do Your Research

Check current hours (may change)
Look up specific items you want
Know your sizes (especially for Traveler’s notebooks)
Make a wishlist



Try Before You Buy

Most stores have test stations
Try mechanical pencils with different grips
Test paper quality in notebooks
Don’t rush



Ask for Recommendations

Staff are knowledgeable and helpful
They love enthusiasts
Ask about new products
Get insider tips



Consider Shipping

Large purchases can be heavy
Some stores ship to hotel
International shipping available at major stores
Check airline luggage limits

title: Git and Branch Workflow for anjins
text:
This document explains how we configured Git authentication in RStudio, created and managed branches (main, chuwi, surface9), and established a workflow for merging changes into main across multiple computers.



GitHub no longer accepts passwords for HTTPS. Instead, we configured a Personal Access Token (PAT) and credential caching for seamless authentication.
git config --global credential.helper store
On the next push or pull:

Username: GitHub username (no email extension)
Password: Your Personal Access Token (PAT)

These credentials are cached in ~/.git-credentials, avoiding repeated prompts.



The repo uses three branches:

main: Stable production branch
chuwi: Work from Chuwi laptop
surface9: Work from Surface 9 device



git checkout -b chuwi
git push -u origin chuwi

git checkout main
git checkout -b surface9
git push -u origin surface9




Each device works in its own branch:
git checkout chuwi      # or surface9
git pull
git add .
git commit -m "Update content from Chuwi"
git push



When ready to merge chuwi or surface9 back into main:
git checkout main
git pull
git merge chuwi         # or surface9
git push



When switching devices:
git checkout chuwi      # or surface9
git pull
Always commit and push your branch before moving to another computer.




Commit and push often to avoid lost work.
Keep main clean and stable.
Pull latest main before merging.
Use meaningful commit messages.




If conflicts occur:

Look for the yellow “C” in the Git pane in RStudio.
Open conflicted files.
Choose “Accept Current” (your branch), “Accept Incoming” (main), or edit manually.
Mark resolved, commit, and push.




main  <--- stable branch
  │
  ├─ chuwi    (work on Chuwi laptop)
  │      \
  │       → merge into main
  │
  └─ surface9 (work on Surface 9)
         \
          → merge into main



git branch                # view local branches
git branch -r             # view remote branches
git checkout branch-name   # switch branches
git checkout -b new-branch # create a new branch
git branch -d old-branch   # delete local branch
git push origin --delete old-branch  # delete remote branch
This workflow ensures smooth development across chuwi and surface9 while keeping main stable.
